questions.hint: Think: having from birth, not taught

questions.hint: Natural abilities are innate — you are born with them

questions.q: Which word is closest in meaning to VERSATILE?

questions.hint: Eclipse as a verb means to outshine or surpass

questions.q: What is the ANTONYM (opposite) of ANTICS?

questions.q: What is the ANTONYM of CAPTIVATE?

questions.hint: To repel is to push people away — the opposite of captivating

questions.hint: Stagnating means staying the same without developing

questions.hint: Innate = from birth; the opposite is something that is taught

questions.q: Choose the word most OPPOSITE to INNATE.

questions.q: What is the ANTONYM of SHROUD (to hide)?
questions.hint: The opposite of hiding something is revealing it

questions.hint: To expose something is to bring it into full view

questions.q: What is the ANTONYM of STIFLE?
questions.hint: Encouragement helps things grow; stifling stops them

questions.q: Choose the word most OPPOSITE to STIFLE.
questions.hint: Releasing allows what stifling holds back

questions.q: What is the ANTONYM of TENTATIVE?

questions.q: Choose the word most OPPOSITE to TENTATIVE.
questions.hint: Firm means decided and not going to change

questions.q: What is the ANTONYM of TRANQUIL?

questions.q: What is the ANTONYM of VERSATILE?

questions.q: A DEFT artist handles the brush with skill; a ___ one fumbles with it.

questions.q: DEFT is to CLUMSY as DILIGENT is to ___.

questions.q: TRANQUIL is to HECTIC as PEACEFUL is to ___.
questions.hint: Tranquil and hectic are opposites; peaceful and chaotic are opposites

questions.q: INNATE is to BORN as LEARNED is to ___.
questions.hint: Innate abilities come at birth; learned ones come through teaching

questions.q: SHROUD is to CONCEAL as REVEAL is to ___.

questions.q: EVOLVE is to CHANGE as STAGNATE is to ___.

questions.q: ATTIRE is to WEDDING as UNIFORM is to ___.

questions.q: VERSATILE is to MANY as SPECIALIZED is to ___.
questions.hint: Versatile means skilled at many things; specialized means one

questions.q: CAPTIVATE is to AUDIENCE as ENCHANT is to ___.

questions.q: DILIGENT is to WORKER as THOROUGH is to ___.

questions.q: DEFT is to JUGGLER as ELOQUENT is to ___.

questions.q: ECLIPSE is to SUN as OVERSHADOW is to ___.

questions.q: STIFLE is to CREATIVITY as POLLUTE is to ___.

questions.q: TENTATIVE is to DEFINITE as UNCERTAIN is to ___.

questions.q: SHROUD is to MYSTERY as VEIL is to ___.

questions.q: INNATE is to TALENT as ACQUIRED is to ___.

questions.q: EVOLVE is to SPECIES as MATURE is to ___.

questions.q: POSTURE is to BODY as EXPRESSION is to ___.

questions.q: TRANQUIL is to SANCTUARY as HECTIC is to ___.

questions.q: DILIGENT is to SUCCESS as CARELESS is to ___.

questions.q: STIFLE is to LAUGHTER as SUPPRESS is to ___.

questions.q: TRANQUIL is to STILL WATER as TURBULENT is to ___.

questions.q: CAPTIVATE is to SPELL as INSPIRE is to ___.

questions.q: ATTIRE is to FASHION as INSCRIPTION is to ___.

questions.q: DILIGENT is to ACHIEVEMENT as NEGLIGENT is to ___.

questions.q: EVOLVE is to BUTTERFLY as MATURE is to ___.

questions.q: Which simile best shows what it means to CAPTIVATE an audience?

ch: a river that carves deeper channels over centuries

ch: a flower that blooms without being taught how

ch: a river that slowly changes its course

ch: a musician who begins before she can walk

ch: a hand placed over a flame before it can grow

questions.q: Complete the METAPHOR: Wang Yani's art was ___ that pulled audiences in from around the world.
questions.hint: A magnet draws things irresistibly — like captivating art draws audiences

questions.q: Complete the METAPHOR: Yani's brush was ___ that spoke without hesitation.
questions.hint: Deft movement is fluid and precise — like a dancer's foot

questions.q: Complete the METAPHOR: Yani's ten years of diligent work were ___ she could never lose.

questions.q: Yani's simple attire at the Smithsonian was ___ that the art alone was enough.

questions.q: The painting that CAPTIVATED the audience was ___ no one could look away from.

questions.q: Wang Yani's innate ability was ___ already burning when she was born.

questions.q: Good posture is ___ that holds confidence upright for the world to see.
questions.hint: Posture is how confidence (or its absence) shows in the body

questions.q: The fog that SHROUDED the harbor was ___ the city had pulled over itself.
questions.hint: A cloak hides — fog shrouds like a cloak wrapped around the city

questions.q: Without freedom to experiment, an artist's creativity is ___ waiting for air.
questions.hint: Stifled creativity is like a flame deprived of what it needs to grow

questions.q: The TENTATIVE opening of a story is ___ before the voice finds its confidence.
questions.hint: Tentative = uncertain first attempt, like clearing your throat before speaking

questions.q: The tranquil sea in Yani's painting was ___ before the storm arrived.
questions.hint: Tranquil calm before a storm is like a breath held in anticipation

questions.q: Wang Yani's versatility made her art ___ that no one genre could contain.
questions.hint: A river moves freely and finds its own path — like versatile art

questions.q: Yani's years of diligent painting were ___ that held up every exhibition she ever gave.
questions.hint: Foundations support everything built above them — as diligence supports achievement

questions.q: When Yani evolved from painting monkeys to landscapes, she was ___ of her art.
questions.hint: Evolving expands what is possible — like adding new territory to a map

questions.q: Yani's talent was ___ that blocked out all that came before her.
questions.hint: Her extraordinary gift overshadowed everything like a solar eclipse

questions.q: An artist's innate sense of color is ___ no teacher could have given.
questions.hint: Innate = given at birth, not earned or awarded

questions.q: A shroud of mystery surrounding a famous painting makes it ___ the art world keeps trying to solve.
questions.hint: A mystery wrapped in a shroud becomes a puzzle — a riddle

questions.q: Criticism that STIFLES a young artist's confidence is ___ that never fully heals.
questions.hint: Stifling does damage — it wounds what it touches

questions.q: Yani's painting of the Guilin mountains was ___ the audience stepped into and could not leave.
questions.hint: Great art is a world — captivating means you live inside it for a moment

questions.q: Which sentence uses APPAREL in a simile correctly?
questions.a: Her flowing apparel moved like butterfly wings in the breeze.
ch: Her flowing apparel moved like butterfly wings in the breeze.
She wore apparel as if furniture had a purpose.
Apparel was the shop that sold everything.
He apparel-ed himself with great speed.
questions.hint: A simile compares using like or as

questions.q: Which sentence uses APPRECIATE in a simile correctly?
questions.a: She appreciated his kindness like a traveler appreciates water in a desert.
ch: She appreciated his kindness like a traveler appreciates water in a desert.
Appreciate was like a store full of discounts.
He appreciated quickly as if running a race.
They appreciated nothing like old furniture.
questions.hint: Think: how much and in what way does someone value something

questions.q: Which sentence uses CONTINUOUS in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The continuous hum of the machines was like a river that never rests.
ch: The continuous hum of the machines was like a river that never rests.

She ran continuously as if she were furniture.
The continuous cost nothing to buy.
questions.hint: Think: something that flows without stopping

questions.q: Which sentence uses DISSOLVE in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The sugar dissolved like a snowflake disappearing on a warm tongue.
ch: The sugar dissolved like a snowflake disappearing on a warm tongue.

Dissolve was like a strong wall.
He dissolved nothing like a firm brick.
questions.hint: Think: how quickly something disappears in liquid

questions.q: Which sentence uses DOMESTICATE in a simile correctly?
questions.a: Taming the wild colt was like trying to domesticate a thunderstorm.
ch: Taming the wild colt was like trying to domesticate a thunderstorm.

She domesticated her pencil like a notebook.
Domesticate was as easy as furniture.
questions.hint: Think: how wild and resistant something is to control

questions.q: Which sentence uses EMERGE in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The silk moth emerged from its cocoon like a secret finally told aloud.
ch: The silk moth emerged from its cocoon like a secret finally told aloud.

She emerged from the store like nothing.
questions.hint: Think: how something comes into view for the first time

questions.q: Which sentence uses FIBER in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The silk fiber was as fine as a strand of morning mist.
ch: The silk fiber was as fine as a strand of morning mist.

The fiber cost nothing like furniture.
questions.hint: Think: how thin and delicate a single thread is

ch: The school cafeteria functions like the heart of a living body, pumping energy to every student.

questions.q: Which sentence uses CONTINUOUS as a metaphor?
questions.a: History is a continuous river, always flowing forward and never turning back.
ch: History is a continuous river, always flowing forward and never turning back.
History flows like a continuous river.
History is as continuous as a river.
questions.hint: Think: equating something abstract directly to something physical

questions.q: Which sentence uses DISSOLVE as a metaphor?
questions.a: All her anger dissolved the moment he smiled at her.
ch: All her anger dissolved the moment he smiled at her.
Her anger melted away like ice.
Her anger was like sugar in warm water.
questions.hint: Think: an emotion being directly described as melting away

questions.q: Which sentence uses DOMESTICATE as a metaphor?
questions.a: Years of city life had domesticated his wild spirit into quiet routine.
ch: Years of city life had domesticated his wild spirit into quiet routine.
City life tamed him like a pet.
He was as domesticated as a household cat.
City life made him more tame over time.
questions.hint: Think: calling a person's spirit directly a wild thing that was tamed

questions.q: Which sentence uses EMERGE as a metaphor?
questions.a: A new leader emerged from the chaos, the calm eye of the storm.
ch: A new leader emerged from the chaos, the calm eye of the storm.
A new leader appeared like sunlight after rain.
A new leader came out of the chaos quickly.
A new leader emerged as if from nowhere.
questions.hint: Think: describing emergence by directly calling it something else

questions.q: Which sentence uses FIBER as a metaphor?
questions.a: Honesty is the fiber of her character, woven into every choice she makes.
ch: Honesty is the fiber of her character, woven into every choice she makes.
She is honest like a strong fiber.
Her honesty is as tough as a thread.
Honesty runs through her like a fiber.
questions.hint: Think: calling a character trait directly a fiber that makes something strong

questions.q: Which sentence uses FUNCTION as a metaphor?
questions.a: The school principal is the function of the entire building, the purpose made human.
ch: The school principal is the function of the entire building, the purpose made human.
The principal serves a purpose like a function.
The principal functions as the leader.
The principal is as important as a function.
questions.hint: Think: calling a person directly the purpose or function of a place

questions.q: Which sentence uses HATCH as a metaphor?
questions.a: Her mind was a nest where brilliant ideas hatched every morning.
ch: Her mind was a nest where brilliant ideas hatched every morning.
Her ideas appeared like hatching chicks.
She hatched ideas like a hen hatches eggs.
Her mind was as creative as a nest.
questions.hint: Think: the mind being called a nest where thoughts are born

questions.q: Which sentence uses INHIBIT as a metaphor?
questions.a: Doubt is the anchor that inhibits the ship of progress.
ch: Doubt is the anchor that inhibits the ship of progress.
Doubt holds you back like an anchor.
Doubt inhibited him as if it were a chain.
questions.hint: Think: calling doubt directly an anchor that holds something back

questions.q: Which sentence uses MINUTE (tiny) as a metaphor?
questions.a: His problems were minute grains of sand on the vast beach of the universe.
ch: His problems were minute grains of sand on the vast beach of the universe.
His problems were as tiny as grains of sand.
His problems were small compared to others.
His problems were like tiny grains of sand.
questions.hint: Think: calling problems directly tiny grains on something vast

questions.q: Which sentence uses MOTION as a metaphor?
questions.a: Change is the motion of civilization, always pushing it forward.
ch: Change is the motion of civilization, always pushing it forward.
Civilization changes as motion carries it.
Change is as constant as motion itself.
questions.hint: Think: calling change directly the motion of something large

questions.q: Which sentence uses SHEATHE as a metaphor?
questions.a: Kindness is a sheath around the sharp edges of her honesty.
ch: Kindness is a sheath around the sharp edges of her honesty.
Her kindness sheathed her honesty like armor.
She was kind as if her honesty were a sword.
Her kindness covered her honesty gently.
questions.hint: Think: calling kindness directly a sheath that protects

questions.q: Which sentence uses SHED as a metaphor?
questions.a: The graduation ceremony was a snake shedding its skin, releasing students into new lives.
ch: The graduation ceremony was a snake shedding its skin, releasing students into new lives.
Graduation shed old identities like a snake.

questions.hint: Think: calling a ceremony directly the act of shedding, without using like or as

questions.q: Which sentence uses TRANSFER as a metaphor?
questions.a: A great book is a transfer of the author's soul into the reader's hands.
ch: A great book is a transfer of the author's soul into the reader's hands.
A great book transfers ideas like a gift.
Reading a book is like a transfer of knowledge.

questions.hint: Think: calling the reading experience directly a soul transfer

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using APPAREL?
questions.a: Autumn dressed the forest in its finest apparel, a coat of red and gold.
ch: Autumn dressed the forest in its finest apparel, a coat of red and gold.
The autumn leaves were like colorful clothing.
The forest wore autumn leaves beautifully.
Autumn made the forest as colorful as a wardrobe.
questions.hint: Think: calling falling leaves directly the forest's clothing

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using CONTINUOUS?
questions.a: Time is a continuous highway with no exits and no turning back.
ch: Time is a continuous highway with no exits and no turning back.
Time moves like a continuous road.
Time passes continuously like a moving car.
Time is as continuous as a river.
questions.hint: Think: calling time directly a road that never ends

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DISSOLVE?
questions.a: The crowd's tension dissolved into laughter when the clown appeared.
ch: The crowd's tension dissolved into laughter when the clown appeared.
The crowd relaxed like a solid melting.
Tension disappeared as if it were dissolved.
The crowd's mood shifted like sugar in water.
questions.hint: Think: describing tension directly dissolving, as if it were a substance in liquid

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using EMERGE?
questions.a: From the wreckage of failure, a stronger version of herself emerged, forged in the fire.
ch: From the wreckage of failure, a stronger version of herself emerged, forged in the fire.
She emerged from failure like a phoenix.
After failure, she appeared stronger than before.
She grew stronger as if emerging from flames.
questions.hint: Think: a person being reborn directly as a new creation

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FIBER?
questions.a: Courage is the fiber that holds the fabric of a hero's character together.
ch: Courage is the fiber that holds the fabric of a hero's character together.
Courage is like a strong fiber in a hero.
A hero is as strong as a woven fiber.
Courage holds a hero together like thread.
questions.hint: Think: calling courage directly a fiber that makes character strong

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FUNCTION?
questions.a: The heart is the function of love made physical, pumping life into every relationship.
ch: The heart is the function of love made physical, pumping life into every relationship.
The heart functions like the engine of love.
Love moves the heart as if it were a machine.
The heart pumps love as if it had a purpose.
questions.hint: Think: calling the heart directly the embodied function of love

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using HATCH?
questions.a: The boardroom was a hatch through which new businesses were born.
ch: The boardroom was a hatch through which new businesses were born.
New ideas hatch in the boardroom like eggs.
The boardroom is as productive as a nest.
New businesses emerge from the boardroom like hatching chicks.
questions.hint: Think: calling a place directly a hatch — an opening for new things

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using INHIBIT?
questions.a: Fear is the padlock that inhibits the door to your full potential.
ch: Fear is the padlock that inhibits the door to your full potential.
Fear holds you back like a padlock.
Fear inhibits progress as if it were a chain.
Fear prevents you from reaching your potential.
questions.hint: Think: calling fear directly a padlock that blocks a door

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using MINUTE (tiny)?
questions.a: Each moment in childhood is a minute jewel, priceless though it seems small.
ch: Each moment in childhood is a minute jewel, priceless though it seems small.
Childhood moments are as small as jewels.
Moments of childhood are tiny and precious like gems.
Childhood is as brief as a tiny jewel.
questions.hint: Think: calling each moment directly a tiny jewel

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using MOTION?
questions.a: Music is the motion of the soul, carrying emotion where words cannot go.
ch: Music is the motion of the soul, carrying emotion where words cannot go.
Music moves the soul like water in motion.
Music moves us emotionally, just like motion.
Music is as moving as a river in flow.
questions.hint: Think: calling music directly the motion of something intangible

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SHEATHE?
questions.a: Sleep is the soft sheath that heals the wounds of a long day.
ch: Sleep is the soft sheath that heals the wounds of a long day.
Sleep covers us like a warm sheath.
Sleep is as protective as a sheath on a blade.

questions.hint: Think: calling sleep directly a sheath that protects and heals

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SHED (to emit light)?
questions.a: Her smile was a candle that shed warm light into every dark corner of the room.
ch: Her smile was a candle that shed warm light into every dark corner of the room.
Her smile shone like a candle in the dark.
Her smile shed light as if it were a lamp.
Her smile was as bright as a candle.
questions.hint: Think: calling her smile directly a candle that emits light

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using TRANSFER?
questions.a: Teaching is the great transfer — a candle lighting other candles without losing its own flame.
ch: Teaching is the great transfer — a candle lighting other candles without losing its own flame.
Teaching transfers knowledge like pouring water.
A teacher is as effective as a transfer of light.
Teaching is like a candle that shares its flame.
questions.hint: Think: calling the act of teaching directly a great transfer of light

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DOMESTICATE?
questions.a: School domesticated his restless curiosity into a focused, disciplined mind.
ch: School domesticated his restless curiosity into a focused, disciplined mind.
School tamed him like a trainer tames an animal.
School changed him as if taming a wild creature.

questions.hint: Think: calling curiosity directly a wild thing that school tamed

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using INHIBIT?
questions.a: Procrastination is the weed that inhibits the garden of achievement.
ch: Procrastination is the weed that inhibits the garden of achievement.
Procrastination holds us back like a weed.
Procrastination inhibits success as if it were a barrier.
Procrastination slows achievement like a slow weed growing.
questions.hint: Think: calling procrastination directly a weed in a garden

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FIBER?
questions.a: Discipline is the invisible fiber that holds a champion's training together day after day.
ch: Discipline is the invisible fiber that holds a champion's training together day after day.
Discipline holds athletes together like a strong fiber.
Discipline is as strong as a fiber in a rope.

questions.hint: Think: calling discipline directly a fiber that holds something together

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using EMERGE?
questions.a: After years of struggle, a master painter emerged from the uncertain student he once was.
ch: After years of struggle, a master painter emerged from the uncertain student he once was.

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using MOTION?
questions.a: Her dancing was a continuous motion of storytelling, each step a paragraph.
ch: Her dancing was a continuous motion of storytelling, each step a paragraph.

ch: Moving to a new city was his chance to shed the old identity that no longer fit.

ch: Every handshake is a transfer of trust, invisible but real.

questions.q: Which sentence uses BRAWL in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The argument erupted like a brawl between two storm fronts colliding in the sky.
ch: The argument erupted like a brawl between two storm fronts colliding in the sky.
The brawl was as quiet as a sleeping dog.
He brawled like a gentle river flowing downstream.
The brawl cost nothing like free sunshine.
questions.hint: Think: comparing a conflict to a sudden violent clash

questions.q: Which sentence uses CASUAL in a simile correctly?
questions.a: He strolled in as casual as a leaf drifting down from a branch.
ch: He strolled in as casual as a leaf drifting down from a branch.
She was casual like a thunderstorm.

questions.q: Which sentence uses ACCOMPANY in a simile correctly?
questions.a: She accompanied the expedition like a compass accompanies a sailor — essential and always present.
ch: She accompanied the expedition like a compass accompanies a sailor — essential and always present.
She accompanied nothing like a closed map.
Accompany was as still as a sleeping river.
She accompanied him as if absent from the journey.
questions.hint: Think: comparing the presence of a person to something always needed

questions.q: Which sentence uses BENEFICIAL in a simile correctly?
questions.a: Sacagawea's knowledge of native plants was as beneficial as a fully stocked medicine chest.
ch: Sacagawea's knowledge of native plants was as beneficial as a fully stocked medicine chest.
The beneficial thing was like an empty bag.
She was beneficial like a broken tool.
Beneficial was as harmful as a sharp knife.
questions.hint: Think: comparing something helpful to a well-equipped resource

questions.q: Which sentence uses CAPTIVE in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The audience sat captive as the storyteller spoke, like birds frozen on a branch by a hawk's shadow.
ch: The audience sat captive as the storyteller spoke, like birds frozen on a branch by a hawk's shadow.
The captive moved freely like a river rushing to the sea.
Captive was as open as a summer field.
She moved like a captive who had no chains.
questions.hint: Think: comparing spellbound attention to creatures unable to move

questions.q: Which sentence uses CONVENIENT in a simile correctly?
questions.a: Having Sacagawea along was as convenient as having a map that could speak.
ch: Having Sacagawea along was as convenient as having a map that could speak.
Convenient was as difficult as hiking through a blizzard.
She was convenient like a locked treasure chest.
The route was convenient as if it were far and treacherous.
questions.hint: Think: comparing ease of travel to a perfect tool that guides you

questions.q: Which sentence uses ECSTASY in a simile correctly?
questions.a: Her ecstasy at reuniting with her brother was like a river finding the sea after years apart.
ch: Her ecstasy at reuniting with her brother was like a river finding the sea after years apart.
Her ecstasy was as cold as a winter storm.
She felt ecstasy like someone discovering bad news.
Ecstasy was as quiet as a stone at the bottom of a lake.
questions.hint: Think: comparing intense joy to two things joyfully reuniting in nature

questions.q: Which sentence uses EXPANSE in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The Pacific lay before her like an expanse of living silver, endless and breathing.
ch: The Pacific lay before her like an expanse of living silver, endless and breathing.
The expanse was as narrow as a footpath.
She crossed the expanse like stepping through a doorway.
Expanse was as confined as a locked room.
questions.hint: Think: comparing the ocean to something vast and shimmering

questions.q: Which sentence uses EXPEDITION in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The Lewis and Clark expedition moved like a slow river pushing toward the unknown.
ch: The Lewis and Clark expedition moved like a slow river pushing toward the unknown.
The expedition stood still like a stopped clock.
Expedition was as short as a quick walk around the block.
The expedition retreated like a tide coming back to shore.
questions.hint: Think: comparing a long, purposeful journey to a force of nature moving forward

questions.q: Which sentence uses INEPT in a simile correctly?
questions.a: Charbonneau's handling of the canoe was as inept as a child trying to juggle boulders.
ch: Charbonneau's handling of the canoe was as inept as a child trying to juggle boulders.
He was inept like a champion swimmer in still water.
His handling was as inept as an expert craftsman's finest work.
Inept was as graceful as a hawk gliding on the wind.
questions.hint: Think: comparing clumsy handling to someone attempting something clearly beyond them

questions.q: Which sentence uses INTERPRET in a simile correctly?
questions.a: Sacagawea interpreted the Shoshone words like a bridge carrying meaning safely from one shore to another.
ch: Sacagawea interpreted the Shoshone words like a bridge carrying meaning safely from one shore to another.
She interpreted as if no words existed between the two sides.
Interpret was as confusing as a silent wall.
She interpreted like someone who knew no language at all.
questions.hint: Think: comparing the act of translating to a structure that connects two separated places

questions.q: Which sentence uses INVALUABLE in a simile correctly?
questions.a: Her knowledge was as invaluable as a lighthouse in a fog-thick sea.
ch: Her knowledge was as invaluable as a lighthouse in a fog-thick sea.
She was invaluable like a cracked compass with no needle.
Invaluable was as worthless as a broken lock.
Her help was invaluable like something easily replaced.
questions.hint: Think: comparing something essential and guiding to a beacon in dangerous conditions

questions.q: Which sentence uses LINGER in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The memory of home lingered in her heart like smoke that stays long after a fire goes out.
ch: The memory of home lingered in her heart like smoke that stays long after a fire goes out.
She lingered like a runner crossing a finish line at full speed.
Linger was as swift as a hawk diving for prey.
She lingered like someone with nowhere to go and nothing to remember.
questions.hint: Think: comparing a persistent memory to something that stays in the air long after its source is gone

questions.q: Which sentence uses RETRIEVE in a simile correctly?
questions.a: She retrieved the records from the river like a hawk retrieving something snatched by the wind.
ch: She retrieved the records from the river like a hawk retrieving something snatched by the wind.
She retrieved nothing like a dog with empty jaws.

She retrieved the papers as if they had never been lost.
questions.hint: Think: comparing fast, purposeful recovery to a bird's precise retrieval

questions.q: Which sentence uses SKIRMISH in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The argument at camp was like a skirmish — brief, loud, and over before anyone knew who won.
ch: The argument at camp was like a skirmish — brief, loud, and over before anyone knew who won.
The skirmish was as quiet as snowfall on a frozen river.
The battle lasted like a skirmish lasts all year.
She skirmished like someone who never took a side.
questions.hint: Think: comparing a short disagreement to a brief military clash

questions.q: Which sentence uses SUPPLEMENT in a simile correctly?
questions.a: She supplemented the food supplies like a gardener adding compost to thin soil.
ch: She supplemented the food supplies like a gardener adding compost to thin soil.
She supplemented like someone who removed everything from the pack.
Supplement was as empty as a bare pantry.
She supplemented the supplies as if taking everything away.
questions.hint: Think: comparing adding to food stores to enriching the ground so things can grow

questions.q: Which sentence uses TERRITORY in a simile correctly?
questions.a: The Shoshone territory was as familiar to Sacagawea as the back of her own hand.
ch: The Shoshone territory was as familiar to Sacagawea as the back of her own hand.
Territory was as unknown to her as a foreign language.
She crossed the territory like someone lost in a maze.
Territory was as unfamiliar as a distant star.
questions.hint: Think: comparing deep knowledge of a place to knowing something intimate and personal

questions.a: a small sun that followed wherever she went
ch: a small sun that followed wherever she went
a heavy stone dragging her to the ground
a locked door blocking every path
a storm cloud gathering on the horizon

ch: a doctor traveling alongside the expedition

questions.q: Complete the simile: Seeing her brother again filled Sacagawea with ecstasy, like ___
questions.a: sunlight flooding a room that had been dark for years
ch: sunlight flooding a room that had been dark for years
a cold wind shutting every door at once
a river running dry before reaching the sea

ch: a key to a door no one else could open

questions.a: an otter snatching something before the river could carry it away
ch: an otter snatching something before the river could carry it away

questions.q: Complete the simile: Moving through Shoshone territory, Sacagawea read the landscape like ___
questions.a: a scholar reads a beloved and well-worn book
ch: a scholar reads a beloved and well-worn book

a child sounds out words they have never seen

questions.q: Complete the simile: The expedition pushed forward into unknown land like ___
questions.a: a single thread being drawn through an endless cloth
ch: a single thread being drawn through an endless cloth
a wide river retreating into the mountains

questions.q: Complete the simile: The smell of the evening campfire lingered like ___
questions.a: a story that refuses to end even after the last page
ch: a story that refuses to end even after the last page

questions.q: Complete the simile: She interpreted the Shoshone chief's words as carefully as ___

ch: a jeweler examines a diamond for hidden flaws

questions.q: Complete the simile: The short clash at the river's edge was like a skirmish compared to ___

questions.q: Which sentence uses ACCOMPANY as a metaphor?
questions.a: Courage accompanied her every step — a silent guardian walking beside her through the wilderness.
ch: Courage accompanied her every step — a silent guardian walking beside her through the wilderness.
Courage went with her like a faithful friend.
She moved with courage as if it were a companion.
Courage was as constant as a shadow by her side.
questions.hint: A metaphor calls something directly another thing without using like or as

questions.q: Which sentence uses BENEFICIAL as a metaphor?
questions.a: Her plant knowledge was the expedition's pharmacy, beneficial beyond any price Lewis and Clark could have paid.
ch: Her plant knowledge was the expedition's pharmacy, beneficial beyond any price Lewis and Clark could have paid.
Her knowledge was as beneficial as a pharmacy.
Her plant skill helped them like a medicine cabinet.
Her knowledge was beneficial the way a doctor is beneficial.
questions.hint: Think: calling her knowledge directly a pharmacy — the thing itself, not a comparison

questions.q: Which sentence uses CAPTIVE as a metaphor?
questions.a: The audience was captive in the storyteller's hands, every soul sealed tight in the amber of her words.
ch: The audience was captive in the storyteller's hands, every soul sealed tight in the amber of her words.
The audience sat captive like insects in amber.
They were as captive as listeners who cannot leave.
The story held them as a captor holds a prisoner.
questions.hint: Think: directly calling the audience captive and their attention an amber that seals them

questions.q: Which sentence uses CONVENIENT as a metaphor?
questions.a: The Hidatsa village was a convenient shelter — the expedition's lucky harbor in a winter sea.
ch: The Hidatsa village was a convenient shelter — the expedition's lucky harbor in a winter sea.
The village was as convenient as a harbor.

questions.q: DAM is to WATER as SAVINGS ACCOUNT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a dam conserves water by holding it; a savings account conserves money the same way

questions.q: CONSERVE is to RESOURCES as PRESERVE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: we conserve natural resources; we preserve food — both extend useful life

questions.q: CONTAMINATE is to DIRTY as PURIFY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: these are opposite processes — one introduces harm, the other removes it

questions.q: CHEMICAL is to WATER as RUMOR is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a chemical contaminates clean water; a rumor contaminates the truth

questions.q: CONTAMINATE is to IMPURITY as PURIFY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: contamination introduces impurity; purification produces cleanliness

questions.q: DIMINISH is to SMALLER as AMPLIFY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: diminish makes things less; amplify makes things more

questions.q: CANDLE is to BURN DOWN as ENTHUSIASM is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a candle burns down to nothing; enthusiasm can diminish the same way if not fed

questions.q: DIMINISH is to SIZE as SOFTEN is to ___
questions.hint: Think: diminishing reduces size; softening reduces intensity

questions.q: DRASTIC is to SEVERE as MILD is to ___
questions.hint: Think: drastic and severe are at one end of the scale; mild and gentle are at the other

questions.q: SURGERY is to DRASTIC as BANDAGE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: surgery is a drastic response to injury; a bandage is a mild one

questions.q: EXTRAVAGANT is to WASTEFUL as FRUGAL is to ___
questions.hint: Think: extravagant describes careless spending; frugal describes careful spending

questions.q: MISER is to HOARDING as SPENDTHRIFT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a miser is extreme in hoarding; a spendthrift is extreme in extravagant spending

questions.q: EXTRAVAGANT is to EXCESS as FRUGAL is to ___
questions.hint: Think: extravagance is the habit of excess; frugality is the habit of economy

questions.q: FRUGAL is to THRIFTY as EXTRAVAGANT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: frugal and thrifty mean the same thing; extravagant pairs with wasteful

questions.q: SQUIRREL is to NUTS as FRUGAL PERSON is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a squirrel stores nuts for winter; a frugal person stores savings for lean times

questions.q: FRUGAL is to SPENDING as CAUTIOUS is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a frugal person is careful about spending; a cautious person is careful about risk

questions.q: IMPURITY is to WATER as ERROR is to ___
questions.hint: Think: an impurity makes water unsafe; an error makes text wrong — both are unwanted additions

questions.q: FILTER is to IMPURITY as EDITOR is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a filter removes impurities from water; an editor removes errors from writing

questions.q: PERIL is to DANGER as SECURITY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: peril and danger are the same thing; security and safety are the same thing

questions.q: LIFEGUARD is to DROWNING as HELMET is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a lifeguard protects against the peril of drowning; a helmet protects against head injury

questions.q: PERPETUAL is to ENDLESS as TEMPORARY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: perpetual describes what never ends; temporary describes what is only briefly present

questions.q: WATER CYCLE is to PERPETUAL as SEASONS are to ___
questions.hint: Think: the water cycle is perpetual — it repeats forever; the seasons are cyclical, returning again and again

questions.q: RESOURCE is to SUPPLY as LIABILITY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a resource is an asset you can draw on; a liability is a burden you must carry

questions.q: LIBRARY is to KNOWLEDGE as RESERVOIR is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a library is a resource for knowledge; a reservoir is a resource for water

questions.q: SUBSTITUTE is to REPLACEMENT as ORIGINAL is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a substitute is a replacement; the original is the authentic thing

questions.q: UNDERSTUDY is to ACTOR as SUBSTITUTE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: an understudy fills in for an actor; a substitute fills in for a teacher

questions.q: SUBSTITUTE is to FILL IN as RETIRE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: to substitute is to step in; to retire is to step down — both are role transitions

questions.q: SUSTAIN is to SUPPORT as UNDERMINE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: sustain holds something up; undermine gradually breaks it down

questions.q: FOOD is to BODY as SUNLIGHT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: food sustains the body; sunlight sustains plant life

questions.q: SUSTAIN is to ONGOING as CEASE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: to sustain something keeps it going; to cease something brings it to an end

questions.q: VITAL is to ESSENTIAL as OPTIONAL is to ___
questions.hint: Think: vital and essential mean the same; optional and unnecessary mean the same

questions.q: OXYGEN is to BREATHING as WATER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: oxygen is vital to breathing; water is vital to life itself

questions.q: VITAL is to ALIVE as LIFELESS is to ___
questions.hint: Think: vital describes someone bursting with life; lifeless describes the complete absence of it

questions.q: Complete the simile: Her worries accumulated like ___
questions.a: leaves piling up in a corner no one bothers to sweep
ch: leaves piling up in a corner no one bothers to sweep
a stone wall someone knocks down every morning
a tide that recedes before it ever reaches the shore
a bucket someone empties before it gets full
questions.hint: Think: small things gathering steadily into a larger and larger mass

questions.q: Complete the simile: The dust on the old piano had accumulated like ___
questions.a: years of silence settling in a layer no one disturbed
ch: years of silence settling in a layer no one disturbed
a fresh breeze that clears the air every morning
a tide that washes away all traces of the day
a quick wipe of a rag that leaves everything spotless
questions.hint: Think: quiet, undisturbed buildup over a very long period

questions.q: Complete the simile: Knowledge accumulates like ___
questions.a: interest in a savings account — slowly at first, then faster and faster
ch: interest in a savings account — slowly at first, then faster and faster
a fire that burns itself out without leaving ash
a wave that erases what came before it
a stone that rolls away and leaves no mark
questions.hint: Think: each addition builds on the last, making the total grow faster over time

questions.q: Complete the simile: His constant interruptions aggravated her concentration like ___
questions.a: a fly buzzing near your ear while you try to sleep
ch: a fly buzzing near your ear while you try to sleep
a gentle breeze on a warm summer afternoon
a soft blanket wrapped around your shoulders
a quiet room with a single candle burning
questions.hint: Think: something small that keeps disrupting what you are trying to do

questions.q: Complete the simile: The extra delay aggravated the situation like ___
questions.a: a second rock dropped into an already cracked windshield
ch: a second rock dropped into an already cracked windshield
a layer of padding placed over a bruise to protect it
a careful bandage applied with clean hands
a warm drink offered to someone who is cold
questions.hint: Think: adding harm to what was already damaged

questions.q: Complete the simile: She conserved water as carefully as ___
questions.a: a desert traveler rationing the last drops in a canteen
ch: a desert traveler rationing the last drops in a canteen
a child splashing through every puddle after rain
someone filling a bathtub and letting it overflow
a farmer who waters crops every hour on the hour
questions.hint: Think: someone who knows exactly how precious their limited supply is

questions.q: Complete the simile: The committee pledged to conserve the forest like ___
questions.a: a museum guard protecting irreplaceable artifacts from harm
ch: a museum guard protecting irreplaceable artifacts from harm
a logger who cuts every tree before winter comes
a builder who clears land for the cheapest possible price
a developer who plants concrete wherever there was soil
questions.hint: Think: careful, vigilant protection of something that cannot be replaced

questions.q: Complete the simile: One factory's waste contaminated the river like ___
questions.a: a single drop of ink turning an entire glass of water gray
ch: a single drop of ink turning an entire glass of water gray
a soft rain that washes the dirt from leaves and stones
a snowfall that covers and purifies everything it touches
a filter that removes every trace of color from the stream
questions.hint: Think: a small amount of harmful substance spreading far beyond where it started

questions.q: Complete the simile: False information contaminated the public debate like ___
questions.a: a weed that spreads underground until it chokes every flower in the garden
ch: a weed that spreads underground until it chokes every flower in the garden
a careful gardener who removes every harmful plant by hand
a clean wind that carries only the seeds of healthy plants
a rain that feeds every plant equally and without harm
questions.hint: Think: something harmful that spreads invisibly and destroys what was healthy

questions.q: Complete the simile: The spilled chemicals contaminated the soil like ___
questions.a: a stain that soaks deeper the longer you wait to treat it
ch: a stain that soaks deeper the longer you wait to treat it
a patch of sunlight that makes the garden grow faster
a gentle frost that disappears when morning comes
a coat of polish that makes old wood shine again
questions.hint: Think: something harmful that penetrates and spreads the longer it is ignored

questions.q: Complete the simile: Her confidence had diminished like ___
questions.a: a once-bright fire reduced to a few glowing embers in the cold
ch: a once-bright fire reduced to a few glowing embers in the cold
a bonfire that roared higher with every passing hour
a flame that burned all the more fiercely in the wind
a blaze that could not be reduced by any amount of rain
questions.hint: Think: something that was once large and powerful, now barely present

questions.q: Complete the simile: The supply of fresh water was diminishing like ___
questions.a: sand slipping through fingers no matter how tightly you hold them
ch: sand slipping through fingers no matter how tightly you hold them
water rising steadily behind a well-built dam
a spring that flowed more strongly with each passing season
a river fed by annual rains that never failed to come
questions.hint: Think: something you cannot stop from slowly disappearing

questions.q: Complete the simile: The new water restrictions were as drastic as ___
questions.a: a surgeon removing an organ to save the body from a worse fate
ch: a surgeon removing an organ to save the body from a worse fate
a doctor who suggests resting and drinking plenty of fluids
a nurse who places a cool cloth on a warm forehead
a pharmacist who recommends a mild over-the-counter remedy
questions.hint: Think: the most severe possible intervention — no half-measures

questions.q: Complete the simile: The change in water quality after the factory opened was as drastic as ___
questions.a: a river that reversed its course overnight
ch: a river that reversed its course overnight
a gentle bend in a stream that smoothed over time
a gradual shift in the current no one noticed for years
a small eddy that spun briefly and then disappeared
questions.hint: Think: a sudden, extreme reversal with no gradual transition

questions.q: Complete the simile: The city's water use during the drought was as extravagant as ___
questions.a: a sailor pouring fresh water overboard in the middle of the ocean
ch: a sailor pouring fresh water overboard in the middle of the ocean
a traveler rationing each drop through a parched desert
a camper boiling and filtering every drop from a mountain stream
a diver surfacing only when absolutely necessary to save air
questions.hint: Think: wasteful behavior in exactly the situation where conservation is most critical

questions.q: Complete the simile: His promises about fixing the water supply were as extravagant as ___
questions.a: a magician who claims he can make it rain gold
ch: a magician who claims he can make it rain gold
a friend who offers only what they know they can deliver
a planner who lists only the steps she is certain she can complete
a scientist who reports only what the data clearly supports
questions.hint: Think: promises far beyond what is realistic or possible to deliver

questions.q: Complete the simile: The desert community was as frugal with water as ___
questions.a: a watchmaker with the tiniest spring in the most delicate clock
ch: a watchmaker with the tiniest spring in the most delicate clock
a child filling a swimming pool one bucket at a time
a gardener who waters everything twice whether it needs it or not
a restaurant that refills your glass before you have taken the first sip
questions.hint: Think: extreme care with something tiny and irreplaceable

questions.q: Complete the simile: She was as frugal with electricity as ___
questions.a: a lighthouse keeper on a remote island who must make his oil last through the winter
ch: a lighthouse keeper on a remote island who must make his oil last through the winter
a child who leaves every light blazing through the night
a family that upgrades to the newest appliances every year
a hotel that never turns off its neon sign
questions.hint: Think: someone who knows supply is limited and treats every unit as precious

questions.q: Complete the simile: The frugal scientist reused every piece of equipment like ___
questions.a: a cobbler who resolves the same boots year after year rather than buying new ones
ch: a cobbler who resolves the same boots year after year rather than buying new ones
a shopper who replaces perfectly good items whenever a new model appears
a cook who throws out leftovers before they can be used again
a builder who tears down a solid wall because it is old
questions.hint: Think: getting the maximum use out of something before replacing it

questions.q: Complete the simile: The rumor spread through the group like an impurity ___
questions.a: dissolving into a clear stream and clouding it beyond recognition
ch: dissolving into a clear stream and clouding it beyond recognition
being filtered out before it could reach any part of the system
trapped behind a barrier that kept it perfectly contained
removed by a careful process that left the water sparkling
questions.hint: Think: something harmful spreading silently through something that was pure

questions.q: Complete the simile: The single defective part was like an impurity ___
questions.a: in a batch of medicine — small but capable of ruining everything
ch: in a batch of medicine — small but capable of ruining everything
in a perfectly sealed container that no outside force could reach
filtered out at every stage before it reached the final product
tested for and eliminated long before it could cause any trouble
questions.hint: Think: one small harmful element that compromises an entire system

questions.q: Complete the simile: Swimming above the waterfall's edge was as foolish as walking into peril ___
questions.a: with a blindfold on and both hands tied behind your back
ch: with a blindfold on and both hands tied behind your back
with every safety line double-checked and every risk assessed
with an experienced guide who knows every dangerous turn
with the right gear and a clear head and a careful plan
questions.hint: Think: walking toward danger while having removed every possible protection

questions.q: Complete the simile: The polluted river was a peril to the town like ___
questions.a: a slow-moving flood that no one could see but everyone would eventually feel
ch: a slow-moving flood that no one could see but everyone would eventually feel
a gentle stream that children wade through without a second thought
a clear mountain brook that cools the air for miles around
a small pond that reflects the sky on a calm summer afternoon
questions.hint: Think: a hidden, creeping danger that builds without warning until it overwhelms

questions.q: Complete the simile: The water cycle runs as perpetually as ___
questions.a: a clock that winds itself and never needs a hand to start it again
ch: a clock that winds itself and never needs a hand to start it again
a machine that breaks down if it runs more than a few hours
a candle that must be relit every time the wind blows it out
a battery that slowly loses its charge no matter how carefully it is stored
questions.hint: Think: a process that repeats endlessly with no outside force needed to restart it

questions.q: Complete the simile: Their argument about water rights seemed as perpetual as ___
questions.a: a tide that recedes only to return before the beach is dry
ch: a tide that recedes only to return before the beach is dry
a single wave that crashes once and leaves the shore in peace
a storm that passes in minutes and is forgotten by noon
a cloud that drifts out of sight and never returns
questions.hint: Think: something that keeps coming back no matter how often it seems to end

questions.q: Complete the simile: Her calm under pressure was a resource like ___
questions.a: a deep underground spring — always there when the surface runs dry
ch: a deep underground spring — always there when the surface runs dry
a shallow pool that evaporates in the first hour of summer heat
a river that floods in spring but disappears by July
a puddle that a child can step over without getting their feet wet
questions.hint: Think: something deeper and more reliable than what appears on the surface

questions.q: Complete the simile: The town treated its forest like a precious resource as carefully as ___
questions.a: a doctor protects the last vial of a medicine that cannot be made again
ch: a doctor protects the last vial of a medicine that cannot be made again
a logger who harvests as quickly as possible before the season ends
a factory that burns raw material to keep the furnace running
a developer who views trees as obstacles to be cleared
questions.hint: Think: protecting something rare and irreplaceable with the utmost care

questions.q: Complete the simile: For the thirsty traveler in the desert, nothing could substitute for water — like ___
questions.a: asking a drowning man if he would prefer a life jacket or a trophy
ch: asking a drowning man if he would prefer a life jacket or a trophy
offering a swimmer extra towels for after the race
giving a rested runner a glass of cool water before the finish line
pouring lemonade for guests who are already satisfied
questions.hint: Think: a situation where only one specific thing will do — no alternative is acceptable

questions.q: Complete the simile: The backup water tank served as a substitute like ___
questions.a: a spare tire — not the best option but absolutely essential when the original fails
ch: a spare tire — not the best option but absolutely essential when the original fails
the primary engine running at full power with no backup needed
the original part that was designed and tested for exactly this role
a component that is preferred over the standard version in every situation
questions.hint: Think: something that fills in adequately when the primary is not available

questions.q: Complete the simile: Her determination sustained the team like ___
questions.a: a keel sustains a ship — unseen but keeping everything upright through the storm
ch: a keel sustains a ship — unseen but keeping everything upright through the storm
a heavy anchor dragging the ship back whenever it tried to move forward
a wave that pushed the boat off its course and toward the rocks
a wind that spun the vessel in circles without direction
questions.hint: Think: a structural element that maintains stability without drawing attention to itself

questions.q: Complete the simile: Clean water sustains life like ___
questions.a: a heartbeat sustains the body — quietly, constantly, and without it everything stops
ch: a heartbeat sustains the body — quietly, constantly, and without it everything stops
a luxury sustains a mood — nice to have, but nothing fails if it disappears
a bonus sustains interest — welcome when it comes, but not relied on
a reward sustains effort — motivating for a moment, then forgotten
questions.hint: Think: not a comfort but a fundamental necessity — remove it and everything collapses

questions.q: Complete the simile: The relief workers sustained hope in the flood-stricken town like ___
questions.a: candles lit one by one in a building where the power has gone out
ch: candles lit one by one in a building where the power has gone out
a power outage spreading through the grid on a cold winter night
a storm that puts out every light and does not pass for days
a blackout that leaves every room cold, dark, and silent
questions.hint: Think: small acts of warmth and light that keep hope alive in darkness

questions.q: Complete the simile: Water is as vital to life as ___
questions.a: a spine is to the body — remove it and nothing holds together
ch: a spine is to the body — remove it and nothing holds together
a scarf is to a coat — nice to have on a cold day but not truly necessary
a second window in a room that already has one
a lamp in a room already lit by the afternoon sun
questions.hint: Think: something so central and structural that life collapses without it

questions.q: Complete the simile: She was as vital to the team as ___
questions.a: an engine is to a ship — take her out and the whole voyage stops
ch: an engine is to a ship — take her out and the whole voyage stops
a flag is to a castle — noticeable and symbolic but not necessary for defense
a coat of paint is to a house — pleasant, but the walls stand without it
a third wheel on a bicycle — an addition that makes balance harder
questions.hint: Think: the central driving force — not decorative, not optional, but core

questions.q: Complete the simile: The conservation plan was as vital as ___
questions.a: a levee before the flood — the time to build it is before you need it, not after
ch: a levee before the flood — the time to build it is before you need it, not after
an umbrella found after the storm has already passed
a map consulted only after arriving at the wrong destination
a lock installed on the door after the house has been emptied
questions.hint: Think: something so necessary that waiting too long to act makes it useless

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ACCUMULATE?
questions.a: Regret is a tax that accumulates silently until the debt comes due all at once.
ch: Regret is a tax that accumulates silently until the debt comes due all at once.
Regret piles up like late fees on an overdue library book.
Regret accumulates the way interest grows in a savings account.
Regret grew as steadily as interest on a loan she had forgotten to pay.
questions.hint: Think: calling regret directly a tax — no like or as — and saying it accumulates

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ACCUMULATE?
questions.a: Each unread email in her inbox was a grain of sand — harmless alone, but accumulated into a dune that buried her workday.
ch: Each unread email in her inbox was a grain of sand — harmless alone, but accumulated into a dune that buried her workday.
Her unread emails piled up like sand dunes in a desert storm.
The emails accumulated as quickly as windblown sand in an open field.
Her inbox filled up the way sand fills a bucket at the beach.
questions.hint: Think: calling each email directly a grain of sand — the inbox IS a dune

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ACCUMULATE?
questions.a: Pollution is a slow accumulation of choices — each one a drop that, together, fills the ocean with regret.
ch: Pollution is a slow accumulation of choices — each one a drop that, together, fills the ocean with regret.
Pollution accumulates the way raindrops fill a barrel left outside.
Pollution piles up like litter along an unswept highway.
The pollution grew like a pile of garbage no one removed.
questions.hint: Think: calling pollution directly an accumulation of choices — the pollution IS the choices

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using AGGRAVATE?
questions.a: Every delay was salt — deliberately aggravating the wound of an already frustrated nation.
ch: Every delay was salt — deliberately aggravating the wound of an already frustrated nation.
Every delay aggravated their frustration the way salt irritates a wound.
Each delay was like pouring salt into a wound that was already painful.
The delays aggravated the public as salt aggravates a burn.
questions.hint: Think: calling each delay directly salt — the delay IS salt being poured on a wound

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using AGGRAVATE?
questions.a: His constant advice was a blister aggravated by every step she took — unwanted, painful, and impossible to ignore.
ch: His constant advice was a blister aggravated by every step she took — unwanted, painful, and impossible to ignore.
His advice aggravated her the way a blister aggravates a long walk.
His unsolicited advice was as aggravating as a blister on a long hike.
His advice felt like a blister that got more painful with every step.
questions.hint: Think: calling his advice directly a blister — the advice IS the wound, aggravated by movement

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using CONSERVE?
questions.a: The rain barrel was a quiet act of conservation — a small savings account for the sky's deposits.
ch: The rain barrel was a quiet act of conservation — a small savings account for the sky's deposits.
The rain barrel helped conserve water the way a savings account conserves money.
Using a rain barrel is like keeping a savings account for rainwater.
A rain barrel conserves water as carefully as a bank conserves your deposits.
questions.hint: Think: calling the barrel directly a savings account — the barrel IS the financial instrument

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using CONSERVE?
questions.a: Conservation is the art of treating every drop as if it were the last one left in the world.
ch: Conservation is the art of treating every drop as if it were the last one left in the world.
Conservation feels like caring for water as though it were incredibly precious.
To conserve water is to treat it like gold — precious and never to be wasted.
Conservation means acting as if every drop were as rare as a diamond.
questions.hint: Think: calling conservation directly an art — the discipline IS the art form

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using CONTAMINATE?
questions.a: Prejudice is a contaminant — invisible in the water of daily conversation until someone falls ill from drinking it.
ch: Prejudice is a contaminant — invisible in the water of daily conversation until someone falls ill from drinking it.
Prejudice contaminates a conversation the way a pollutant contaminates a river.
Prejudice spreads like a toxin through water, invisible until the damage is done.
Prejudice can contaminate a group as silently as chemicals contaminate a well.
questions.hint: Think: calling prejudice directly a contaminant — not comparing it, saying it IS one

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using CONTAMINATE?
questions.a: The factory was a contamination engine — running day and night, pumping poison into the veins of the watershed.
ch: The factory was a contamination engine — running day and night, pumping poison into the veins of the watershed.
The factory contaminated the watershed the way an engine pollutes the air.
The factory ran like a contamination machine that never shut down.
The factory's output contaminated the river as steadily as an engine burns fuel.
questions.hint: Think: calling the factory directly an engine — the factory IS the contamination machine

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using CONTAMINATE?
questions.a: Doubt is a contaminant in the mind — once it enters the clear water of confidence, the whole supply becomes unsafe.
ch: Doubt is a contaminant in the mind — once it enters the clear water of confidence, the whole supply becomes unsafe.
Doubt contaminates confidence the way pollution contaminates drinking water.
Doubt creeps into confidence like bacteria into a water supply.
Once it enters, doubt spreads through confidence as toxins spread through water.
questions.hint: Think: calling doubt directly a contaminant — no like or as — and the mind is the water supply

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DIMINISH?
questions.a: Each lie he told was a chisel — diminishing the statue of his reputation until only rubble remained.
ch: Each lie he told was a chisel — diminishing the statue of his reputation until only rubble remained.
Each lie diminished his reputation the way a chisel reduces a block of stone.
His lies chipped away at his reputation like a sculptor's chisel on marble.
His reputation diminished as steadily as a stone block under a sculptor's tools.
questions.hint: Think: calling each lie directly a chisel — the lie IS the tool that diminishes

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DIMINISH?
questions.a: Clean water reserves are a shrinking inheritance — diminished by each generation that takes more than it gives back.
ch: Clean water reserves are a shrinking inheritance — diminished by each generation that takes more than it gives back.
Clean water reserves diminish the way an inheritance shrinks when heirs overspend.
Clean water is like an inheritance that each generation uses up a little more.
The water supply shrinks like an inheritance divided among too many heirs.
questions.hint: Think: calling water reserves directly an inheritance — the reserves ARE the inheritance being diminished

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DRASTIC?
questions.a: The new water law was a surgeon's saw — drastic, irreversible, and cutting through the habits of a generation.
ch: The new water law was a surgeon's saw — drastic, irreversible, and cutting through the habits of a generation.
The new law was drastic, like a surgeon's saw cutting through tissue.
The law felt as drastic as a surgical cut — precise and impossible to undo.
The law was as drastic as surgery — cutting deep and leaving permanent change.
questions.hint: Think: calling the law directly a saw — the law IS the surgical instrument

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DRASTIC?
questions.a: Cutting water use in half was a drastic amputation — the city lost a limb but lived.
ch: Cutting water use in half was a drastic amputation — the city lost a limb but lived.
Cutting water use in half was as drastic as an amputation.
The water cuts were so drastic it felt like cutting off a limb.
Reducing water use so drastically was like performing an amputation on the city.
questions.hint: Think: calling the water cut directly an amputation — the reduction IS the medical procedure

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using EXTRAVAGANT?
questions.a: Their lawn was an extravagant mouth, drinking thousands of gallons a week while the aquifer ran dry beneath them.
ch: Their lawn was an extravagant mouth, drinking thousands of gallons a week while the aquifer ran dry beneath them.
Their lawn consumed water as extravagantly as an open mouth drinks it in.
Maintaining that lawn was like feeding an extravagant mouth that never stopped eating.
The lawn was so extravagant in its water use that it seemed to drink like an open mouth.
questions.hint: Think: calling the lawn directly a mouth — the lawn IS the devouring, extravagant consumer

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using EXTRAVAGANT?
questions.a: The resort's fountain was an extravagant theater — performing for guests while the village upstream went without.
ch: The resort's fountain was an extravagant theater — performing for guests while the village upstream went without.
The resort's fountain was as extravagant as a theater performance — impressive but wasteful.
The fountain performed like an extravagant show that cost far more than it was worth.
Maintaining that fountain was as wasteful as running a theater no one needed.
questions.hint: Think: calling the fountain directly a theater — the fountain IS the performance, the extravagance

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FRUGAL?
questions.a: Her water use was a ledger — every drop entered, accounted for, and never spent without a reason.
ch: Her water use was a ledger — every drop entered, accounted for, and never spent without a reason.
Her water use was as frugal as a miser keeping a ledger of every penny.
She was so frugal with water that she kept track of every drop like a bookkeeper.
Her approach to water was like keeping a tight financial ledger — nothing wasted.
questions.hint: Think: calling her water use directly a ledger — the practice IS the accounting system

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FRUGAL?
questions.a: The frugal farmer's fields were a conversation with the land — taking only what was offered and giving back what was taken.
ch: The frugal farmer's fields were a conversation with the land — taking only what was offered and giving back what was taken.
The frugal farmer worked his fields as carefully as someone who listens as much as they speak.
Farming frugally was like having a conversation — you take only what is given and return what you receive.
His frugal farming was like a dialogue with nature, give and take in equal measure.
questions.hint: Think: calling the fields directly a conversation — the cultivation IS the dialogue

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FRUGAL?
questions.a: The frugal city was a closed fist — gripping its water supply through drought and flood alike.
ch: The frugal city was a closed fist — gripping its water supply through drought and flood alike.
The frugal city held its water supply as tightly as a closed fist holds what it contains.
The city was as frugal as a clenched fist — never letting go of what it had stored.
Being frugal about water was like making a fist and refusing to let anything slip through.
questions.hint: Think: calling the city directly a closed fist — the city IS the gripping hand, frugal and unyielding

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using IMPURITY?
questions.a: Every secret they kept from each other was an impurity — a particle that slowly clouded the water of their trust.
ch: Every secret they kept from each other was an impurity — a particle that slowly clouded the water of their trust.
Every secret was like an impurity slowly clouding the water of their friendship.
Their secrets contaminated their trust the way impurities cloud water.
Their hidden truths were like impurities that made the water of their relationship murky.
questions.hint: Think: calling each secret directly an impurity — no like or as — the secret IS the contaminant

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using IMPURITY?
questions.a: Greed is an impurity in the economy — a substance that makes the whole system toxic when it accumulates unchecked.
ch: Greed is an impurity in the economy — a substance that makes the whole system toxic when it accumulates unchecked.
Greed acts like an impurity in the economy, slowly poisoning the whole system.
When greed accumulates, it contaminates the economy the way impurities contaminate water.
Greed spreads through the economy like an impurity through clean water.
questions.hint: Think: calling greed directly an impurity — the economic force IS the contaminant

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using PERIL?
questions.a: Every mile of unprotected coastline was a peril waiting to speak — a threat the tide would eventually translate into catastrophe.
ch: Every mile of unprotected coastline was a peril waiting to speak — a threat the tide would eventually translate into catastrophe.
Every mile of unprotected coastline was as full of peril as a trap set and never sprung.
Each unprotected mile of coast was like a sleeping danger waiting to wake.
The coastline was full of peril, like a hidden trap waiting to be triggered.
questions.hint: Think: calling each mile directly a peril — the geography IS the threat, waiting to deliver its message

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using PERIL?
questions.a: Polluted water is a silent peril — it offers itself as refreshment and delivers poison.
ch: Polluted water is a silent peril — it offers itself as refreshment and delivers poison.
Polluted water is as perilous as a cup that looks clean but contains poison.
Drinking polluted water is like walking into a danger that disguises itself as safety.
Contaminated water is a peril that looks harmless, like a cup of cool, clear poison.
questions.hint: Think: calling the water directly a peril — the water IS the danger itself

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using PERPETUAL?
questions.a: The water cycle is a perpetual conversation between the sky and the sea — one always answering the other's call.
ch: The water cycle is a perpetual conversation between the sky and the sea — one always answering the other's call.
The water cycle is as perpetual as a conversation between two parties who never stop talking.
The cycle between sky and sea is so perpetual it resembles an endless exchange.
The water cycle perpetually moves between sky and sea like an endless conversation.
questions.hint: Think: calling the cycle directly a conversation — the water cycle IS the dialogue, and it is perpetual

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using PERPETUAL?
questions.a: Their dispute over water rights was a perpetual machine — fueled by old grievances that never burned down.
ch: Their dispute over water rights was a perpetual machine — fueled by old grievances that never burned down.
Their dispute was as perpetual as a machine that never stops running.
The dispute ran perpetually, like a motor fueled by grievances that would not burn out.
Their disagreement was like a perpetual-motion machine, driven by grudges that never ran out.
questions.hint: Think: calling the dispute directly a machine — the conflict IS the perpetual engine

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using RESOURCE?
questions.a: The river was the town's only true resource — not a convenience but the spine around which every life was built.
ch: The river was the town's only true resource — not a convenience but the spine around which every life was built.
The river was such a vital resource that it was like the backbone of the community.
The river served as a resource the way a spine serves a body — essential and structural.
Without the river as a resource, the town would be as unstable as a body without a spine.
questions.hint: Think: calling the river directly the spine — the river IS the structural resource that everything else depends on

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using RESOURCE?
questions.a: Her creativity was an inexhaustible resource — a spring that flowed more abundantly the more it was drawn upon.
ch: Her creativity was an inexhaustible resource — a spring that flowed more abundantly the more it was drawn upon.
Her creativity was like an inexhaustible spring that flowed more the more you drew from it.
She was resourceful in the way a deep spring is — always producing more when asked.
Her creative ability was as inexhaustible as a spring that never runs dry.
questions.hint: Think: calling creativity directly a resource and then a spring — the abstract quality IS the water supply

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SUBSTITUTE?
questions.a: Compromise is the substitute we choose when the original is no longer available — imperfect but the only option on the shelf.
ch: Compromise is the substitute we choose when the original is no longer available — imperfect but the only option on the shelf.
Compromise functions like a substitute for the solution we actually wanted.
We turn to compromise the way we use a substitute when the real thing is unavailable.
Compromise is as imperfect as a substitute — something we choose when we can't have the original.
questions.hint: Think: calling compromise directly a substitute — the act IS the replacement product

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SUBSTITUTE?
questions.a: Recycled water is not a substitute — it is the original, cleaned and returned to service.
ch: Recycled water is not a substitute — it is the original, cleaned and returned to service.
Recycled water is not so much a substitute as it is a restored version of the original.
Recycled water isn't really a substitute — it's more like the original given a second chance.
Using recycled water isn't substituting — it's restoring what was already there.
questions.hint: Think: making an argument about identity using substitute — defining what recycled water IS and IS NOT

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SUSTAIN?
questions.a: Water is the earth's bloodstream — it sustains every living thing the way blood sustains every cell.
ch: Water is the earth's bloodstream — it sustains every living thing the way blood sustains every cell.
Water sustains the earth the way blood sustains a body.
The role of water in sustaining the earth is like the role of blood in a living body.
Water sustains life the way blood does — circulating through systems and feeding every part.
questions.hint: Think: calling water directly the bloodstream — it IS the circulatory system, and it sustains like blood

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SUSTAIN?
questions.a: Hope was the raft that sustained them — not comfortable, not safe, but the only thing keeping them above the flood.
ch: Hope was the raft that sustained them — not comfortable, not safe, but the only thing keeping them above the flood.
Hope sustained them the way a raft sustains a swimmer — imperfect but essential.
They were sustained by hope the way a swimmer is sustained by a raft in rough water.
Hope kept them going the way a raft keeps a person from going under in a flood.
questions.hint: Think: calling hope directly a raft — the emotion IS the floating device that sustains them

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SUSTAIN?
questions.a: The cheers of the crowd were oxygen — sustaining the runner through the final mile when her legs had nothing left.
ch: The cheers of the crowd were oxygen — sustaining the runner through the final mile when her legs had nothing left.
The crowd's cheers sustained her the way oxygen sustains a runner's muscles.
She was sustained by the crowd's cheering as a runner is sustained by a fresh supply of oxygen.
The cheers worked like oxygen, sustaining her energy when her body had nearly given out.
questions.hint: Think: calling the cheers directly oxygen — the emotional support IS the gas that sustains physical life

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using VITAL?
questions.a: Water is not a resource — it is a vital organ, and we have been slowly removing it from the body of the earth.
ch: Water is not a resource — it is a vital organ, and we have been slowly removing it from the body of the earth.
Water is so vital that removing it from the earth is like removing an organ from a body.
Without water, the earth is as vulnerable as a body without one of its vital organs.
Destroying the water supply is as dangerous as removing a vital organ from a living creature.
questions.hint: Think: calling water directly a vital organ — not comparing it to one, but saying it IS one, embedded in the earth's body

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using VITAL?
questions.a: She was the vital spark of the project — without her energy, the whole engine would have gone cold.
ch: She was the vital spark of the project — without her energy, the whole engine would have gone cold.
She was so vital to the project that losing her would be like losing a spark in an engine.
Her contribution was as vital to the project as a spark is to an engine.
The project depended on her the way an engine depends on a spark to keep running.
questions.hint: Think: calling her directly a spark — the person IS the vital ignition source

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using VITAL?
questions.a: Conservation is not an option — it is the vital sign that tells us whether the planet is still alive.
ch: Conservation is not an option — it is the vital sign that tells us whether the planet is still alive.
Conservation is as vital a sign of planetary health as a heartbeat is of a living person.
The conservation rate is as vital to the earth's health as a vital sign is to a patient.
Like a doctor checking vital signs, we must check conservation rates to know if the planet is healthy.
questions.hint: Think: calling conservation directly a vital sign — the practice IS the diagnostic indicator of planetary life
