questions.q: ANTICIPATE is to FUTURE as REMEMBER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: anticipate looks forward; remember looks backward

questions.q: HUNTER is to TRACKS as FORECASTER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a hunter reads tracks to predict prey; a forecaster reads signs to anticipate weather

questions.q: ANTICIPATE is to EXPECT as DREAD is to ___
questions.hint: Think: anticipate describes expecting something good; dread describes expecting something bad

questions.q: BANKRUPT is to WEALTH as EXHAUSTED is to ___
questions.hint: Think: bankrupt means completely out of money; exhausted means completely out of energy

questions.q: DEBT is to BANKRUPT as DROUGHT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: too much debt leads to bankruptcy; too little rain leads to something parched

questions.q: BANKRUPT is to INSOLVENT as FRUGAL is to ___
questions.hint: Think: bankrupt and insolvent mean the same; frugal and thrifty mean the same

questions.q: BRIEF is to SHORT as LENGTHY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: brief and short mean the same; lengthy and long mean the same

questions.q: BRIEF is to PROLONGED as FAD is to ___
questions.hint: Think: brief (short-lived) is opposite to prolonged; a fad is opposite to a classic

questions.q: NOVEL is to PAGES as BRIEF is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a novel contains many pages; a brief contains only the main points

questions.q: BRISK is to SLUGGISH as ENERGETIC is to ___
questions.hint: Think: brisk and energetic are similar; their opposites are sluggish and tired

questions.q: BRISK is to PACE as REFRESHING is to ___
questions.hint: Think: we describe a pace as brisk and a wind as refreshing — both imply something invigorating

questions.q: BUDGET is to SPENDING as SCHEDULE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a budget controls spending; a schedule controls time

questions.q: BUDGET is to OVERSPEND as PLAN is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a budget is broken when you overspend; a plan is broken when you deviate from it

questions.q: COMPETE is to VICTORY as STUDY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: you compete in hopes of victory; you study in hopes of knowledge

questions.q: COMPETITOR is to RIVAL as ALLY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a competitor IS your rival; an ally IS your partner

questions.q: COMPETE is to CONTEST as COLLABORATE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: you compete in a contest; you collaborate on a project

questions.q: COMPLICATE is to SIMPLE as CLARIFY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: to complicate makes something NOT simple; to clarify makes something NOT confusing

questions.q: KNOT is to ROPE as COMPLICATION is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a knot complicates a rope; a complication tangles up a plan

questions.q: COMPLICATE is to DIFFICULT as SIMPLIFY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: to complicate something makes it difficult; to simplify something makes it easy

questions.q: EFFECT is to CAUSE as RESULT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: an effect follows a cause; a result follows a reason

questions.q: MEDICINE is to CURE as EFFORT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: medicine produces a cure; effort produces an effect

questions.q: EFFECTIVE is to SUCCESS as INEFFECTIVE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: an effective approach leads to success; an ineffective approach leads to failure

questions.q: ERR is to MISTAKE as EXCEL is to ___
questions.hint: Think: to err produces a mistake; to excel produces an achievement

questions.q: ERRONEOUS is to WRONG as ACCURATE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: erroneous and wrong mean the same; accurate and right mean the same

questions.q: FACTOR is to RESULT as INGREDIENT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a factor contributes to a result the way an ingredient contributes to a finished dish

questions.q: FACTOR is to OUTCOME as CAUSE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a factor helps produce an outcome; a cause produces an effect

questions.q: FAD is to BRIEF as TRADITION is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a fad is brief by definition; a tradition is enduring by definition

questions.q: SUMMER is to FAD as CENTURY is to ___

questions.q: FAD is to FORGOTTEN as CLASSIC is to ___

questions.q: GRIPE is to COMPLAINT as PRAISE is to ___

questions.q: GRIPE is to DISSATISFIED as CHEER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: you gripe when you are dissatisfied; you cheer when you are pleased

questions.q: KNACK is to SKILL as FLAW is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a knack is a skill or strength; a flaw is a weakness

questions.q: KNACK is to EASE as STRUGGLE is to ___

questions.q: ARTIST is to KNACK as ATHLETE is to ___

questions.q: LEISURE is to WORK as VACATION is to ___

questions.q: LEISURE is to RELAXED as LABOR is to ___

questions.q: LEISURELY is to BRISK as SLOW is to ___

questions.q: UNIQUE is to SINGULAR as COMMON is to ___

questions.q: FINGERPRINT is to PERSON as UNIQUE IDEA is to ___

questions.q: UNIQUE is to ORIGINAL as ORDINARY is to ___

questions.q: Complete the simile: She anticipated the results like ___

ch: a child awake before dawn on Christmas morning — ears straining for the first creak on the stair
a student who forgot the test was happening until she walked in the door
an adult who hears the news after everyone else and shrugs
a worker who arrives late and misses the announcement entirely
questions.hint: Think: eager, wide-awake anticipation — every sense tuned to the coming moment

questions.q: Complete the simile: The coach anticipated every opponent's move like ___
questions.a: a chess player who sees twelve moves ahead before lifting a piece
ch: a chess player who sees twelve moves ahead before lifting a piece
a beginner who moves whatever piece is nearest without a plan
a player who stares at the board until the timer runs out
a gambler who chooses by flipping a coin
questions.hint: Think: seeing possible futures and preparing for them before they arrive

questions.q: Complete the simile: He anticipated the crowd's reaction like ___
questions.a: a comedian who knows exactly where the laugh will land before he delivers the line
ch: a comedian who knows exactly where the laugh will land before he delivers the line
an actor who trips over his lines and recovers by accident
a speaker who forgets what he was going to say halfway through
a performer who is surprised every single time the audience laughs
questions.hint: Think: not just predicting but preparing for what comes next with quiet confidence

questions.q: Complete the simile: After years of criticism, his confidence was as bankrupt as ___
questions.a: a store that has sold every item on its shelves and cannot restock
ch: a store that has sold every item on its shelves and cannot restock
a warehouse so full of inventory that the doors can barely close
a shop stocked with everything a customer could ever want
a pantry that gets restocked every time one item runs low
questions.hint: Think: completely emptied out — nothing left to draw on

questions.q: Complete the simile: The company went bankrupt as suddenly as ___
questions.a: a candle blown out by a single unexpected gust
ch: a candle blown out by a single unexpected gust
a fire built carefully over hours until it is warm and steady
a lamp switched on when the electricity is restored
a flame sheltered in glass that burns for days without a flicker
questions.hint: Think: a sudden, total ending with no gradual warning

questions.q: Complete the simile: Fame for most toy inventors was as brief as ___
questions.a: a soap bubble — beautiful, fragile, and gone before you can point it out to someone else
ch: a soap bubble — beautiful, fragile, and gone before you can point it out to someone else
a mountain carved by a glacier that stands ten thousand years
a tradition so old that no one alive remembers when it started
a river that flows steadily through every season without change
questions.hint: Think: fleeting, beautiful, and impossible to hold onto once it begins to fade

questions.q: Complete the simile: The toy's time at the top of the charts was as brief as ___
questions.a: a shooting star — blazing and brilliant and then completely dark
ch: a shooting star — blazing and brilliant and then completely dark
a sunrise that lasts all day and gently becomes a golden afternoon
a lighthouse beam that sweeps the coast reliably every few seconds
a tide that goes out but always faithfully returns to the shore
questions.hint: Think: spectacular but over almost before you could appreciate it

questions.q: Complete the simile: The inventor's briefing lasted as briefly as ___
questions.a: a handshake — over almost before it has begun
ch: a handshake — over almost before it has begun
a speech that runs past midnight and still has not reached its conclusion
a lecture that fills every seat and spills into the hallway
a meeting where everyone speaks for an hour before anything is decided
questions.hint: Think: the shortest possible version of something that normally takes much longer

questions.q: Complete the simile: The game's opening pace was as brisk as ___
questions.a: the first five minutes of a race when every runner still has full energy
ch: the first five minutes of a race when every runner still has full energy
the last mile of a marathon when legs are heavy and lungs are burning
the moment a runner crosses the finish line and collapses on the grass
the water break in the middle of a long training session
questions.hint: Think: fast, energetic, full of early power before fatigue sets in

questions.q: Complete the simile: Her brisk efficiency in the meeting was like ___
questions.a: a surgeon who wastes no movement and finishes an hour ahead of schedule
ch: a surgeon who wastes no movement and finishes an hour ahead of schedule
a speaker who takes forty minutes to introduce herself before her first point
a committee that meets to discuss whether to schedule a discussion
a chair who apologizes for the length and then adds another thirty minutes
questions.hint: Think: precise, fast, no wasted motion — everything done at the right speed

questions.q: Complete the simile: She managed her game-production budget like ___
questions.a: a tightrope walker who knows that every inch to either side ends in a fall
ch: a tightrope walker who knows that every inch to either side ends in a fall
a child spending a holiday gift card with no plan and no limit
a traveler who buys whatever catches his eye without checking his wallet
a shopper who doubles every price in the store because the discount seems wrong
questions.hint: Think: precise balance — any deviation in either direction has serious consequences

questions.q: Complete the simile: The start-up company ran on a budget as tight as ___
questions.a: a new shoe that has not yet been broken in
ch: a new shoe that has not yet been broken in
a suit tailored with extra room in case the wearer gains weight
a glove made two sizes too large to allow for thick winter liners
a costume that fits every member of the cast regardless of size
questions.hint: Think: pressing in from every side — uncomfortably restrictive but holding its shape

questions.q: Complete the simile: The toy companies competed as fiercely as ___
questions.a: gladiators in an arena where only one brand can leave with the season's profits
ch: gladiators in an arena where only one brand can leave with the season's profits
old friends who take turns winning so no one is ever disappointed
teammates who share credit equally and help each other past the finish line
dancers at a recital who cheer for one another from the wings
questions.hint: Think: no mercy, no sharing — the market is a winner-take-all contest

questions.q: Complete the simile: She competed for the invention prize like ___
questions.a: a marathon runner who has trained for this one race for three years
ch: a marathon runner who has trained for this one race for three years
a spectator who wanders onto the course not knowing a race is in progress
a volunteer who is only there to hand out water at the halfway point
a runner who signs up the morning of the race with no training
questions.hint: Think: long preparation, total commitment — this moment is what everything was aimed at

questions.q: Complete the simile: The new copyright rules complicated the inventor's plan like ___
questions.a: a road closure that turns a ten-minute trip into a two-hour detour
ch: a road closure that turns a ten-minute trip into a two-hour detour
a shortcut that shaves twenty minutes off an already easy journey
a highway with no traffic and a speed limit you are welcome to exceed
a map that gives you exactly the route you hoped for
questions.hint: Think: a simple path suddenly made much longer and harder to navigate

questions.q: Complete the simile: Adding more rules to the game complicated it like ___
questions.a: extra knots tied on an already knotted rope
ch: extra knots tied on an already knotted rope
a final polish that makes an already smooth surface easier to slide across
a coat of paint that hides the rough spots and unifies the whole design
a label that makes a confusing product instantly clear to any buyer
questions.hint: Think: adding more complexity to what was already hard to untangle

questions.q: Complete the simile: The advertisement had an effect on toy sales like ___
questions.a: a spark landing in dry grass — small in the beginning, enormous very quickly
ch: a spark landing in dry grass — small in the beginning, enormous very quickly
a bucket of water poured carefully over a fire that has nearly gone out
a gentle rain that slowly raises the level of a very deep reservoir
a single brick added carefully to a wall that is already nearly complete
questions.hint: Think: a small action triggering an outsized, rapidly-spreading result

questions.q: Complete the simile: The new safety rule effected a change in the factory like ___
questions.a: a new conductor arriving to an orchestra — everything adjusts to a different rhythm
ch: a new conductor arriving to an orchestra — everything adjusts to a different rhythm
a single musician who plays the wrong note and is quickly corrected
a melody that sounds identical whether the conductor is present or not
a symphony that proceeds exactly as it did before, note for note
questions.hint: Think: a single authoritative force reshaping how the whole organization moves

questions.q: Complete the simile: The company erred in its forecast like ___
questions.a: a navigator who reads a map upside down and announces they are headed north
ch: a navigator who reads a map upside down and announces they are headed north
a pilot who checks all instruments twice before announcing altitude
a captain who anchors in the harbor and waits for better weather
a scout who studies the terrain and returns with an accurate report
questions.hint: Think: confident in a conclusion that is completely wrong because the input was flawed

questions.q: Complete the simile: To err in business strategy is as costly as ___
questions.a: a ship's captain who plots the wrong heading and only notices three days out to sea
ch: a ship's captain who plots the wrong heading and only notices three days out to sea
a navigator who double-checks every coordinate before leaving the harbor
a pilot who adjusts course the moment instruments suggest a drift
a captain who calls for updates every hour and corrects immediately
questions.hint: Think: the longer you travel in the wrong direction, the more it costs to return

questions.q: Complete the simile: Timing was a factor in the toy's success like ___
questions.a: the wind is a factor in a kite — invisible until it lifts everything or brings it crashing down
ch: the wind is a factor in a kite — invisible until it lifts everything or brings it crashing down
the string on a kite — always present, always visible, always in full control
the tail on a kite — decorative and noticeable but not responsible for flight
the kite's color — attractive but entirely unrelated to whether it rises or falls
questions.hint: Think: an invisible force that determines the outcome without being the obvious star

questions.q: Complete the simile: The inventor identified every factor in the game's success as carefully as ___
questions.a: a scientist listing every variable before beginning an experiment
ch: a scientist listing every variable before beginning an experiment
a poet who writes whatever comes to mind and trusts it to be right
a painter who skips the sketch and goes straight to the final canvas
a chef who adds ingredients by feel rather than by any measured recipe
questions.hint: Think: systematic, careful accounting of everything that plays a role

questions.q: Complete the simile: The new game caught on as quickly as a fad ___
questions.a: spreading through a middle school the Monday after a popular kid discovers it
ch: spreading through a middle school the Monday after a popular kid discovers it
growing steadily in popularity over decades until every household has a copy
building a loyal following one careful convert at a time over many years
earning its place in history through consistent quality and quiet staying power
questions.hint: Think: explosive, immediate, tied entirely to social contagion rather than lasting value

questions.q: Complete the simile: The toy died out as quickly as a fad ___
questions.a: abandoned the moment the next thing arrived — no one could even remember why they had wanted it
ch: abandoned the moment the next thing arrived — no one could even remember why they had wanted it
retired after decades of use, still fondly remembered by everyone who played with it
passed on from generation to generation like a family recipe or a well-loved story
replaced only after it had served its purpose so completely that nothing was left to improve
questions.hint: Think: vanishing without a trace the instant something newer appears

questions.q: Complete the simile: Chasing fads in the toy business is like ___
questions.a: trying to catch a wave — by the time you paddle into position, it has already passed beneath you
ch: trying to catch a wave — by the time you paddle into position, it has already passed beneath you
surfing a long, perfect swell that carries you all the way to shore without effort
swimming in a steady current that always flows in the same reliable direction
floating on a calm surface where nothing moves and nothing surprises you
questions.hint: Think: speed matters — the opportunity is gone almost before it is visible

questions.q: Complete the simile: His constant gripes about the rules were like ___
questions.a: a dripping faucet — minor each time, but impossible to ignore after a while
ch: a dripping faucet — minor each time, but impossible to ignore after a while
a burst pipe that floods everything immediately and demands instant action
a perfectly sealed tap that allows nothing through no matter what the pressure
a fountain that adds to the atmosphere without causing any inconvenience
questions.hint: Think: small and persistent — each instance is minor but the accumulation wears you down

questions.q: Complete the simile: She delivered her gripe as calmly as ___
questions.a: a lawyer presenting a brief — measured, organized, and entirely without drama
ch: a lawyer presenting a brief — measured, organized, and entirely without drama
an actor building toward a climactic scene with maximum emotional effect
a child having a tantrum in the cereal aisle on a Saturday morning
a protester shouting at the top of their voice until the crowd responds
questions.hint: Think: a complaint delivered with professional restraint and precise language

questions.q: Complete the simile: She had a knack for inventing games like ___
questions.a: a chef who opens the fridge, sees five random ingredients, and produces a masterpiece
ch: a chef who opens the fridge, sees five random ingredients, and produces a masterpiece
a student who needs the recipe written out in full before beginning any dish
a cook who reads every review before deciding whether to add salt
an assistant who prefers to watch someone else cook before ever trying it alone
questions.hint: Think: creative instinct that turns ordinary materials into something remarkable without apparent effort

questions.q: Complete the simile: His knack for explaining complicated rules was like ___
questions.a: a translator who hears a tangle of words and instantly returns perfect clarity
ch: a translator who hears a tangle of words and instantly returns perfect clarity
a student who rereads the passage six times and still is not sure what it means
a speaker who replaces every simple word with a longer and harder one
a teacher who leaves out the key step and cannot understand why nobody follows
questions.hint: Think: something difficult made effortless through natural ability

questions.q: Complete the simile: The veteran inventor's knack for spotting winning ideas was like ___
questions.a: a truffle hunter's nose — unerring, inexplicable, and far better than any technology
ch: a truffle hunter's nose — unerring, inexplicable, and far better than any technology
a metal detector that beeps for everything including bottle caps and loose change
a compass that works only when it is already pointing in the right direction
a map that shows every road including the ones that no longer exist
questions.hint: Think: an instinct so finely tuned it works when logic and analysis would fail

questions.q: Complete the simile: Their weekend leisure felt as rare as ___
questions.a: a clear day in a city that has been covered in fog for a month
ch: a clear day in a city that has been covered in fog for a month
a daily alarm that goes off at the same time every morning without fail
a scheduled break that falls at the same reliable point in every working week
a sunset that happens every evening whether or not anyone is watching
questions.hint: Think: something beautiful that almost never comes, making it precious when it does

questions.q: Complete the simile: He moved through his leisure time like ___
questions.a: a river through a wide, flat plain — unhurried, spreading gently into every corner
ch: a river through a wide, flat plain — unhurried, spreading gently into every corner
a mountain stream that tumbles down rapids and never slows for a breath
a waterfall that drops fifty feet in under a second and crashes at the bottom
a rainstorm that passes in fifteen minutes and leaves everything rearranged
questions.hint: Think: slow, meandering, in no hurry to arrive anywhere in particular

questions.q: Complete the simile: Finding time for leisure after a busy product launch was like ___
questions.a: surfacing for air after a very long dive — urgently needed and deeply satisfying
ch: surfacing for air after a very long dive — urgently needed and deeply satisfying
stepping into a pool you were not expecting to get wet in
wading through water that is colder than it looked from the shore
splashing through a puddle in shoes that were not meant to get wet
questions.hint: Think: a necessary break from a sustained effort — relief and restoration combined

questions.q: Complete the simile: The inventor's idea was as unique as ___
questions.a: a fingerprint — no two alike in all the world, and no way to fake one
ch: a fingerprint — no two alike in all the world, and no way to fake one
a factory stamp placed on every product to show it came from the same machine
a serial number shared by items that rolled off the same production line
a design copied so many times that the original cannot be traced
questions.hint: Think: absolutely individual — not similar to anything else, identifiable as only itself

questions.q: Complete the simile: The toy's design was as unique as ___
questions.a: a one-of-a-kind sculpture — the moment you copied it, the original was no longer unique
ch: a one-of-a-kind sculpture — the moment you copied it, the original was no longer unique
a template used to stamp out thousands of identical pieces
a mold that produces the same shape every single time it is used
a blueprint copied and distributed to every factory in the country
questions.hint: Think: not just different but the absolute only one — irreproducible and irreplaceable

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ANTICIPATE?
questions.a: The inventor's excitement was a hound on a scent — nose down, already anticipating the prize before the hunt had begun.
ch: The inventor's excitement was a hound on a scent — nose down, already anticipating the prize before the hunt had begun.
He was as eager as a hunting dog that already anticipated the prize.
The inventor anticipated the prize the way a hound anticipates the scent.
Like a dog that has caught the scent, the inventor anticipated success before the hunt was over.
questions.hint: Think: calling the inventor's excitement directly a hound — no like or as — already anticipating

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ANTICIPATE?
questions.a: Hope is a forecast — always anticipating the sunshine even when the clouds are piling up.
ch: Hope is a forecast — always anticipating the sunshine even when the clouds are piling up.
Hope is like a weather forecast that anticipates sunshine even when clouds gather.
He anticipated the best the way a hopeful forecaster predicts sunshine despite clouds.
She anticipated success the way a sunny forecast anticipates a beautiful afternoon.
questions.hint: Think: calling hope directly a forecast — no like or as — it IS the prediction of something good

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ANTICIPATE?
questions.a: Every good game designer is a mind reader — anticipating what the player will try before the player knows it themselves.
ch: Every good game designer is a mind reader — anticipating what the player will try before the player knows it themselves.
A good game designer anticipates the player's moves like a mind reader would.
The designer was as good at anticipating players' choices as a skilled mind reader.
Like a mind reader, the designer anticipated moves that players had not yet considered.
questions.hint: Think: calling the designer directly a mind reader — anticipating others' thoughts is the defining skill

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BANKRUPT?
questions.a: The fad was a sunset — dazzling for a moment, then bankrupt of color, leaving only the dark.
ch: The fad was a sunset — dazzling for a moment, then bankrupt of color, leaving only the dark.
The fad faded as quickly as a sunset, bankrupt of color almost before it arrived.
Like a sunset, the fad was bankrupt of its brilliant colors by the time night fell.
The fad disappeared like a sunset — brilliant first, then bankrupt of all its brightness.
questions.hint: Think: calling the fad directly a sunset — it IS the visual experience of going suddenly bankrupt of color

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BANKRUPT?
questions.a: The company was a hollow shell — bankrupt not just of money but of ideas, leadership, and will.
ch: The company was a hollow shell — bankrupt not just of money but of ideas, leadership, and will.
The company was as bankrupt of ideas as a hollow shell with nothing left inside.
Like a hollow shell, the bankrupt company had nothing left inside it.
The company reminded us of a hollow shell — empty and bankrupt in every possible way.
questions.hint: Think: calling the company directly a hollow shell — the company IS the empty container, bankrupt of everything

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BRIEF?
questions.a: Every fad is a comet — brief, brilliant, and impossible to hold.
ch: Every fad is a comet — brief, brilliant, and impossible to hold.
Every fad is as brief as a comet passing through the sky.
Like a comet, a fad has only a brief time in the spotlight.
A fad comes and goes as briefly as a comet crossing the night sky.
questions.hint: Think: calling every fad directly a comet — the fad IS the brief, dazzling object passing through the sky

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BRIEF?
questions.a: The toy's fame was a spark — brief, bright, and swallowed by the dark before anyone had quite finished admiring it.
ch: The toy's fame was a spark — brief, bright, and swallowed by the dark before anyone had quite finished admiring it.
The toy's fame was as brief as a spark — gone almost as soon as it appeared.
Like a spark, the toy's fame was brief and bright before disappearing.
The toy's fame was so brief it was like watching a spark ignite and go out.
questions.hint: Think: calling the toy's fame directly a spark — the fame IS the brief, bright combustion

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BRIEF?
questions.a: Her briefing to the board was a knife — brief, precise, and cutting straight to the point.
ch: Her briefing to the board was a knife — brief, precise, and cutting straight to the point.
Her briefing was as brief and precise as a sharp knife cutting to the point.
Like a knife, her presentation was brief and got straight to what mattered.
She briefed the board as quickly as a knife cuts — brief and straight to the point.
questions.hint: Think: calling the briefing directly a knife — the presentation IS the cutting instrument

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BRISK?
questions.a: The new CEO was a cold front — brisk, energizing, and leaving everyone sharper and more focused in her wake.
ch: The new CEO was a cold front — brisk, energizing, and leaving everyone sharper and more focused in her wake.
The new CEO was as brisk as a cold front that refreshed everyone it passed through.
Like a cold front, the CEO was brisk and left everyone feeling more focused.
She was brisk in her style, like a cold front that sweeps through and refreshes the air.
questions.hint: Think: calling the CEO directly a cold front — she IS the brisk weather event that changes the atmosphere

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BRISK?
questions.a: Holiday shopping season was a brisk tide — pulling everyone in before they could decide whether they wanted to go.
ch: Holiday shopping season was a brisk tide — pulling everyone in before they could decide whether they wanted to go.
Holiday shopping season was as brisk as a tide that pulls everyone toward the shore.
Like a brisk tide, the holiday shopping season swept everyone up in its current.
The holiday shopping season moved briskly, like a tide pulling shoppers toward the stores.
questions.hint: Think: calling the shopping season directly a tide — it IS the brisk current that pulls people along

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BUDGET?
questions.a: The company's budget was a fence — every dollar on the wrong side of it was a loss that could not be undone.
ch: The company's budget was a fence — every dollar on the wrong side of it was a loss that could not be undone.
The budget was like a fence — cross it and you have gone too far.
Like a fence, the budget marked the boundary between safe spending and dangerous excess.
The budget functioned like a fence, keeping the company's expenses within the safe zone.
questions.hint: Think: calling the budget directly a fence — the number IS the boundary that separates safety from ruin

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BUDGET?
questions.a: Their advertising budget was a loaded cannon — not much use if you aimed it wrong.
ch: Their advertising budget was a loaded cannon — not much use if you aimed it wrong.
Their advertising budget was like a cannon — powerful if aimed correctly.
Like a cannon, their budget was only useful if pointed at the right target.
Their budget, like a loaded cannon, had to be aimed properly to have any effect.
questions.hint: Think: calling the budget directly a cannon — the money IS the weapon, and aim determines outcome

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using COMPETE?
questions.a: The holiday toy market was a gladiatorial arena — every brand a competitor fighting for the child's final attention.
ch: The holiday toy market was a gladiatorial arena — every brand a competitor fighting for the child's final attention.
The holiday toy market was like a gladiatorial arena where brands competed for children's attention.
Like gladiators, toy companies competed in the holiday market for the children's choice.
The toy market was so competitive it resembled a gladiatorial arena where only one could win.
questions.hint: Think: calling the market directly an arena — the commercial space IS the battlefield where competitors fight

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using COMPETE?
questions.a: Every inventor was a runner on the same track — competing not just for the prize but for the right to stay in the race.
ch: Every inventor was a runner on the same track — competing not just for the prize but for the right to stay in the race.
Every inventor competed like a runner on the same track, fighting for position.
Like runners, inventors competed with one another for the chance to win.
Each inventor competed as fiercely as a runner who knows only the first finisher will be noticed.
questions.hint: Think: calling each inventor directly a runner — the person IS the competitor in the foot race, not compared to one

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using COMPLICATE?
questions.a: Adding a third manufacturer was a wrench thrown into the gears — complicating a process that had been running smoothly.
ch: Adding a third manufacturer was a wrench thrown into the gears — complicating a process that had been running smoothly.
Adding a third manufacturer was like throwing a wrench into well-running gears.
Like a wrench in the gears, the third manufacturer complicated the process.
The third manufacturer was as disruptive as a wrench thrown into the machinery.
questions.hint: Think: calling the new manufacturer directly a wrench — the decision IS the thing that jams the mechanism

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using COMPLICATE?
questions.a: The patent dispute was a tangle of wire — the more they pulled at it, the more complicated it became.
ch: The patent dispute was a tangle of wire — the more they pulled at it, the more complicated it became.
The patent dispute was as complicated as a tangle of wire that gets worse when you pull it.
Like a tangle of wire, the patent dispute became more complicated the more they tried to resolve it.
The dispute was so complicated it felt like pulling a tangle of wire that only tightened.
questions.hint: Think: calling the dispute directly a tangle — the legal conflict IS the knotted wire that resists untangling

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using EFFECT?
questions.a: The right advertisement is a key — it effects an opening in the consumer's attention that nothing else can unlock.
ch: The right advertisement is a key — it effects an opening in the consumer's attention that nothing else can unlock.
The right advertisement is like a key that effects an opening in the consumer's attention.
Like a key, the right advertisement effects an unlocking of attention in the consumer.
The advertisement worked like a key, effecting an opening that no other approach could make.
questions.hint: Think: calling the advertisement directly a key — the ad IS the key, and it effects an opening

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using EFFECT?
questions.a: A skilled inventor's pitch is a stone dropped in still water — the effects spread outward long after the stone has sunk.
ch: A skilled inventor's pitch is a stone dropped in still water — the effects spread outward long after the stone has sunk.
A skilled inventor's pitch is like a stone dropped in water — the effects ripple outward.
Like a stone in still water, the pitch sent effects rippling outward in every direction.
The pitch spread its effects the way a stone sends ripples across still water.
questions.hint: Think: calling the pitch directly a stone — the presentation IS the stone, and its effects are the ripples

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ERR?
questions.a: To err in a product launch is to fire a cannon in the wrong direction — the energy is real, but the target was never yours to hit.
ch: To err in a product launch is to fire a cannon in the wrong direction — the energy is real, but the target was never yours to hit.
To err in a product launch is like firing a cannon in the wrong direction.
Like a misfired cannon, erring in a product launch sends energy in the wrong direction.
An error in a product launch is as costly as a cannon shot aimed at the wrong target.
questions.hint: Think: calling the error directly the act of misfiring — no like or as, just the equation itself

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ERR?
questions.a: Their erroneous forecast was a false map — confidently drawn, cheerfully followed, and pointing nowhere near the destination.
ch: Their erroneous forecast was a false map — confidently drawn, cheerfully followed, and pointing nowhere near the destination.
Their erroneous forecast was like a false map that confidently pointed in the wrong direction.
Like a false map, their erroneous forecast led them away from rather than toward success.
The forecast was so erroneous it functioned like a map drawn for the wrong country.
questions.hint: Think: calling the forecast directly a false map — the error IS the misdirection, confidently delivered

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FACTOR?
questions.a: Timing is not just a factor — it is the tide, and you either launch with it or you are left stranded on the sand.
ch: Timing is not just a factor — it is the tide, and you either launch with it or you are left stranded on the sand.
Timing is like a tide — a crucial factor in whether your launch succeeds.
Like a tide, timing is the kind of factor that determines whether you float or strand.
Timing is such a crucial factor that it is almost like a tide pulling products to success or failure.
questions.hint: Think: calling timing directly the tide — not a factor in the normal sense but the tide itself

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FACTOR?
questions.a: Price was the silent factor at every meeting — an invisible hand on the scale that tipped every decision.
ch: Price was the silent factor at every meeting — an invisible hand on the scale that tipped every decision.
Price was like a silent factor — an invisible force that influenced every decision made.
Like a hidden weight on a scale, price was the factor that tipped every decision.
Price was such a powerful factor in every meeting that it was like an invisible hand on the scale.
questions.hint: Think: calling price directly a hand — the abstract factor IS the physical agent tipping the balance

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FAD?
questions.a: Every fad is a bonfire on the beach — the crowd gathers fast, burns through the night, and by morning there is only ash.
ch: Every fad is a bonfire on the beach — the crowd gathers fast, burns through the night, and by morning there is only ash.
Every fad is like a bonfire — it draws a crowd and burns brightly before going out.
Like a beach bonfire, a fad draws everyone in quickly and burns through its fuel fast.
A fad is as brief as a beach bonfire — exciting while it lasts but gone by morning.
questions.hint: Think: calling every fad directly a bonfire — the fad IS the fire, and it burns itself out exactly like one

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FAD?
questions.a: The toy that captured the world's attention was not a classic — it was a shooting star, a fad that lit up and burned out in a single season.
ch: The toy that captured the world's attention was not a classic — it was a shooting star, a fad that lit up and burned out in a single season.
The toy was like a shooting star fad — bright for a moment and then gone.
Like a shooting star, the toy was the kind of fad that lights up and disappears quickly.
The toy burned out as fast as a shooting star, confirming what many suspected about its fad status.
questions.hint: Think: calling the toy directly a shooting star and then a fad — the toy IS the brief celestial event

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using FAD?
questions.a: Fashion is a river of fads — always moving, always bringing something new downstream, always carrying the old things away.
ch: Fashion is a river of fads — always moving, always bringing something new downstream, always carrying the old things away.
Fashion is like a river of fads, always carrying new things in and old things away.
Like a river, fashion brings fads downstream and carries them away when they are spent.
Fashion moves like a river — fads arrive from upstream and are carried away just as quickly.
questions.hint: Think: calling fashion directly a river — the entire industry IS the flowing water, and fads are what it carries

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using GRIPE?
questions.a: His gripe was a stuck gear — grinding against every smooth operation, slowing what should have moved freely.
ch: His gripe was a stuck gear — grinding against every smooth operation, slowing what should have moved freely.
His gripe was like a stuck gear that ground against the smooth operation of the team.
Like a stuck gear, his constant gripes slowed down what should have moved freely.
His gripe functioned like a stuck gear, grinding against the otherwise smooth process.
questions.hint: Think: calling the gripe directly a stuck gear — the complaint IS the mechanical obstruction

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using GRIPE?
questions.a: The employees' gripes were a signal flare — ignored for months, then suddenly impossible to overlook when they lit the sky.
ch: The employees' gripes were a signal flare — ignored for months, then suddenly impossible to overlook when they lit the sky.
The employees' gripes were like signal flares that eventually became impossible to ignore.
Like signal flares, the gripes were ignored for months before they lit up the sky.
Their gripes were as hard to ignore as a signal flare once they finally sent them up.
questions.hint: Think: calling the gripes directly a signal flare — the complaints ARE the distress signal, ignored then impossible to miss

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using KNACK?
questions.a: She had a knack for the business that was a compass needle — always finding true north while others spun in circles.
ch: She had a knack for the business that was a compass needle — always finding true north while others spun in circles.
She had a knack for finding the right direction like a compass needle always pointing north.
Like a compass needle, her knack for business always pointed her in the right direction.
Her knack for business was as reliable as a compass needle that always finds true north.
questions.hint: Think: calling her knack directly a compass needle — the skill IS the navigation instrument

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using KNACK?
questions.a: His knack for spotting trends was a sixth sense — processing what others could not see and delivering verdicts before the data arrived.
ch: His knack for spotting trends was a sixth sense — processing what others could not see and delivering verdicts before the data arrived.
His knack for spotting trends was like a sixth sense that gave him data before anyone else had it.
Like a sixth sense, his knack for trend-spotting gave him information that others simply could not access.
He had such a knack for spotting trends that it was almost as if he possessed a sixth sense.
questions.hint: Think: calling the knack directly a sixth sense — the skill IS the extra-perceptual ability, not merely like one

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using KNACK?
questions.a: Her knack for game design was a skeleton key — opening every locked door between an idea and a finished product.
ch: Her knack for game design was a skeleton key — opening every locked door between an idea and a finished product.
Her knack for game design was like a skeleton key that opened every locked door.
Like a skeleton key, her knack for design could open any obstacle between idea and product.
She had such a knack for design that it functioned like a skeleton key, opening everything.
questions.hint: Think: calling her knack directly a skeleton key — the skill IS the master key that unlocks all obstacles

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using LEISURE?
questions.a: Free time was a foreign country to her — leisure was a language she had never been taught to speak.
ch: Free time was a foreign country to her — leisure was a language she had never been taught to speak.
Free time was as foreign to her as a country she had never visited and whose language she did not know.
Like a foreign country, her free time was a kind of leisure she found difficult to navigate.
She approached her leisure time the way a traveler approaches a foreign country — unfamiliar and unpracticed.
questions.hint: Think: calling free time directly a foreign country and leisure a language — leisure IS the unknown territory

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using LEISURE?
questions.a: Sunday afternoon was the only pocket of leisure in a week that was otherwise a grinding machine.
ch: Sunday afternoon was the only pocket of leisure in a week that was otherwise a grinding machine.
Sunday afternoon provided her with leisure in a week that was as relentless as a grinding machine.
Like a brief pocket of air in a week that ground her down, Sunday afternoon offered leisure.
Her Sunday leisure was as welcome as a rest stop in a week as relentless as a grinding machine.
questions.hint: Think: calling the week directly a grinding machine — the non-leisure time IS the mechanized, relentless process

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using LEISURE?
questions.a: For the veteran toy inventor, leisure was a well — deep, refreshing, and visited only when the creative supply had run dry.
ch: For the veteran toy inventor, leisure was a well — deep, refreshing, and visited only when the creative supply had run dry.
For the inventor, leisure was like a well — visited when creativity ran dry and left refreshed.
Like a well, leisure was something the inventor returned to when she needed to restore her creative supply.
Her leisure time was so refreshing it was like drawing water from a very deep and cool well.
questions.hint: Think: calling leisure directly a well — the free time IS the water source that replenishes creative work

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using UNIQUE?
questions.a: A truly original game is a unicorn — unique, hunted by every company, and almost certainly a myth until you actually find one.
ch: A truly original game is a unicorn — unique, hunted by every company, and almost certainly a myth until you actually find one.
A truly original game is as unique as a unicorn — something every company hunts for but rarely finds.
Like a unicorn, a truly original game is unique and incredibly rare.
A truly original game is so unique it might as well be a unicorn for all the luck companies have finding one.
questions.hint: Think: calling the original game directly a unicorn — the rare product IS the mythical creature

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using UNIQUE?
questions.a: Her design was a fingerprint — unique, unreplicable, and belonging to no one but her.
ch: Her design was a fingerprint — unique, unreplicable, and belonging to no one but her.
Her design was as unique as a fingerprint — impossible to copy exactly.
Like a fingerprint, her design was unique and could not be replicated by anyone else.
The design was so unique it was almost like a fingerprint — completely impossible to duplicate.
questions.hint: Think: calling her design directly a fingerprint — the creation IS the unique biological identifier

questions.q: ABANDON is to LEAVE as SUPPORT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: abandon = leave (give up); support = stay (continue helping)

questions.q: CAPTAIN is to ABANDON SHIP as SOLDIER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a captain abandons a ship; a soldier deserts a post — same act in different contexts

questions.q: ABANDON is to FORSAKE as MAINTAIN is to ___
questions.hint: Think: abandon = forsake (synonyms); maintain = keep (synonyms) — parallel pairs

questions.q: ADVERSARY is to ENEMY as ALLY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: adversary = enemy; ally = friend — antonym pairs

questions.q: TROY is to ADVERSARY as GREECE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: Troy and Greece were adversaries — each was the other's opponent

questions.q: ADVERSARY is to OPPONENT as TEAMMATE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: adversary = opponent (against you); teammate = partner (with you)

questions.q: BAFFLE is to CONFUSE as CLARIFY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: baffle = confuse (make harder to understand); clarify = explain (make easier to understand)

questions.q: RIDDLE is to BAFFLE as SOLUTION is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a riddle baffles; a solution solves — each word acts on the related concept

questions.q: BAFFLED is to PERPLEXED as ENLIGHTENED is to ___
questions.hint: Think: baffled = perplexed (confused); enlightened = informed (made clear) — synonyms in each pair

questions.q: BLUNDER is to MISTAKE as TRIUMPH is to ___
questions.hint: Think: blunder = mistake; triumph = success — antonym pairs

questions.q: CHESS PLAYER is to BLUNDER as SURGEON is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a chess player blunders; a surgeon makes an error — both are serious mistakes in their fields

questions.q: COLOSSAL is to ENORMOUS as TINY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: colossal = enormous (both mean very large); tiny = miniature (both mean very small)

questions.q: ELEPHANT is to COLOSSAL as MOUSE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: an elephant is colossal; a mouse is tiny — each animal matches its size word

questions.q: COLOSSAL is to SIZE as DEAFENING is to ___
questions.hint: Think: colossal describes extreme size; deafening describes extreme sound — each is an extreme of its category

questions.q: DETECT is to DISCOVER as OVERLOOK is to ___
questions.hint: Think: detect = discover (find something); overlook = miss (fail to find something)

questions.q: DETECTIVE is to DETECT as SCIENTIST is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a detective's job is to detect; a scientist's job is to research — professional roles

questions.q: DETECT is to NOTICE as IGNORE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: detect = notice (see something); ignore = miss (not see something) — parallel meanings

questions.q: HAUL is to PULL as SHOVE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: haul = pull (draw toward you); shove = push (force away from you)

questions.q: FISHERMAN is to HAUL NETS as FARMER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a fisherman hauls nets; a farmer harvests crops — each gathers the results of their labor

questions.q: OVERPOWER is to DEFEAT as SURRENDER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: overpower = defeat (win); surrender = yield (give up) — antonym pairs

questions.q: STRENGTH is to OVERPOWER as WEAKNESS is to ___
questions.hint: Think: strength enables you to overpower; weakness leads to surrender

questions.q: GREEK SOLDIERS is to OVERPOWER as TROJANS is to ___
questions.hint: Think: the Greek soldiers overpowered the guards; the Trojans yielded — the Trojan War outcome

questions.q: REJOICE is to JOY as MOURN is to ___
questions.hint: Think: to rejoice is the expression of joy; to mourn is the expression of grief

questions.q: VICTORY is to REJOICE as DEFEAT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: victory causes rejoicing; defeat causes despair — cause and emotional effect

questions.q: SCOFF is to MOCK as PRAISE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: scoff = mock (show contempt); praise = honor (show respect) — antonym pairs

questions.q: CASSANDRA is to SCOFF AT as WISDOM is to ___
questions.hint: Think: the Trojans scoffed at Cassandra; they dismissed wisdom — same action in different terms

questions.q: SCOFF is to RIDICULE as ADMIRE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: scoff = ridicule (show contempt); admire = respect (show esteem) — antonym pairs

questions.q: SENTINEL is to GUARD as PRISONER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a sentinel is a guard (watches the gate); a prisoner is a captive (kept inside)

questions.q: SENTINEL is to WATCH as WORKER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a sentinel's job is to watch; a worker's job is to labor — role and action

questions.q: SENTINEL AT GATE is to TROY as SECURITY GUARD is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a sentinel guards Troy's gate; a security guard watches a building's entrance — same function, different settings

questions.q: SIEGE is to SURROUND as RETREAT is to ___
questions.hint: Think: siege = surround (move in); retreat = withdraw (pull back) — opposite military movements

questions.q: SIEGE is to CITY as BLOCKADE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a siege surrounds a city; a blockade surrounds a port — same strategy, different targets

questions.q: SINISTER is to EVIL as BENIGN is to ___
questions.hint: Think: sinister = evil; benign = harmless — antonym pairs

questions.q: SINISTER LOOK is to FEAR as WARM SMILE is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a sinister look causes fear; a warm smile causes comfort — expression and emotional response

questions.q: VILLAIN is to SINISTER as HERO is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a villain is sinister; a hero is noble — each character matches their defining quality

questions.q: VICTOR is to WINNER as LOSER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: victor = winner; loser = defeated — antonym pairs

questions.q: VICTOR is to SPOILS as LOSER is to ___
questions.hint: Think: the victor wins the spoils (rewards); the loser receives defeat — outcomes of winning and losing

questions.q: GREECE is to VICTOR as TROY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: Greece was the victor of the Trojan War; Troy was vanquished (defeated)

questions.q: WOE is to MISERY as JOY is to ___
questions.hint: Think: woe = misery; joy = happiness — antonym pairs

questions.q: BLUNDER is to WOE as SUCCESS is to ___
questions.hint: Think: a blunder leads to woe; a success leads to joy — cause and emotional result

a captain steering into the storm to keep everyone safe

ch: a musician who hears a single instrument slightly out of tune in a full orchestra

ch: a ship being dragged over a sandbar — inch by reluctant inch

ch: a trumpet blast swallowing the sound of every quieter instrument

ch: diners turning away an unfamiliar dish before tasting it — dismissed without a moment's consideration
