morning mist rising from a field in the early light
steam from a kettle on a cold morning
fog lifting off the water as the sun comes up
questions.hint: Think: sinister = dark, curling, suggesting harm — like smoke from a fire whose purpose isn't warmth

questions.q: Complete the simile: The abandoned house looked as sinister as ___
questions.a: a smile with too many teeth — technically right, somehow deeply wrong
ch: a smile with too many teeth — technically right, somehow deeply wrong
a window with the light on and a warm fire visible inside
a door left open in welcome on a familiar street
a garden tended carefully with flowers blooming in the right season
questions.hint: Think: sinister = looks like something familiar but feels wrong — like a smile that is just slightly off

questions.q: Complete the simile: The victor stood above the fallen city like ___
questions.a: a storm that has spent itself and left only ruin behind
ch: a storm that has spent itself and left only ruin behind
a warm front moving in after a long cold snap
a gentle rain that settles the dust without breaking anything
a wind that stirs the trees without tearing any branches loose
questions.hint: Think: victor after destruction = power that has fully discharged — like a spent storm that has nothing left but the wreckage

questions.q: Complete the simile: She emerged from the competition victorious like ___
questions.a: a phoenix stepping out of its own ashes — something destroyed transformed into something triumphant
ch: a phoenix stepping out of its own ashes — something destroyed transformed into something triumphant
a candle blown out by the first gust of wind that found it
a spark that flares once and is gone before anyone can see it
a flame that needs constant tending or it quietly goes out on its own
questions.hint: Think: victor after a hard fight = renewed, triumphant — like a phoenix, the struggle IS the transformation

questions.q: Complete the simile: The Trojans' woe spread through the city like ___
questions.a: smoke from a fire they had lit themselves — impossible to escape what they had caused
ch: smoke from a fire they had lit themselves — impossible to escape what they had caused
a morning fog that burns off cleanly the moment the sun appears
rain that washes everything clean and leaves the air fresh afterward
a wind that carries seeds and brings new growth wherever it blows
questions.hint: Think: woe caused by your own blunder = inescapable — like smoke from a fire you lit

questions.q: Complete the simile: Her woe was as visible as ___
questions.a: a bruise on a pale face — impossible to miss, impossible to pretend wasn't there
ch: a bruise on a pale face — impossible to miss, impossible to pretend wasn't there
a mark hidden under clothing that no one outside could see
a thought kept entirely inside, never showing on the face or body
a feeling so quiet that only the person carrying it could feel its weight
questions.hint: Think: woe shows — like a bruise, it marks the surface and everyone can see it

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ABANDON?
questions.a: Trust, once abandoned, is a bridge burned from both sides — you cannot cross back over it, and neither can anyone else.
ch: Trust, once abandoned, is a bridge burned from both sides — you cannot cross back over it, and neither can anyone else.
Trust, once abandoned, is as hard to rebuild as a bridge that has been burned.
Like a bridge burned from both sides, trust once abandoned becomes impossible to cross.
Abandoning trust is as destructive as burning a bridge you both needed to use.
questions.hint: Think: calling abandoned trust directly a burned bridge — it IS the destroyed crossing, and abandonment IS the fire

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ABANDON?
questions.a: Hope was the one thing the trapped miners refused to abandon — their last lantern in a collapsed shaft.
ch: Hope was the one thing the trapped miners refused to abandon — their last lantern in a collapsed shaft.
Hope felt like a lantern to the miners, and they refused to abandon it.
Like a lantern they refused to let go of, the miners held on to hope and refused to abandon it.
The miners refused to abandon hope, which burned like a lantern in the darkness.
questions.hint: Think: calling hope directly a lantern — it IS their light source, the one thing they refuse to leave behind

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ADVERSARY?
questions.a: Time was the Trojans' most patient adversary — it wore them down not with swords but with ten years of waiting.
ch: Time was the Trojans' most patient adversary — it wore them down not with swords but with ten years of waiting.
Time acted like an adversary to the Trojans — patient, wearing them down without weapons.
Like an adversary more patient than any soldier, time wore down the Trojans over ten years.
Time was as much an adversary to the Trojans as any Greek soldier — slow, relentless, exhausting.
questions.hint: Think: calling time directly an adversary — it IS their enemy, the one that defeats them with patience

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using ADVERSARY?
questions.a: Doubt is every dreamer's adversary — a small, persistent voice that grows louder every time you hesitate.
ch: Doubt is every dreamer's adversary — a small, persistent voice that grows louder every time you hesitate.
Doubt works like an adversary for dreamers — it speaks against them every time they hesitate.
Like an adversary that never rests, doubt speaks against you every time you consider giving up.
Doubt is as much an adversary to dreamers as any real opponent — it fights against them from within.
questions.hint: Think: calling doubt directly an adversary — it IS the enemy, fighting from inside rather than outside

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BAFFLE?
questions.a: The wooden horse was a riddle the Trojans never solved — it baffled them, and that bafflement was the trap.
ch: The wooden horse was a riddle the Trojans never solved — it baffled them, and that bafflement was the trap.
The wooden horse baffled the Trojans the way a riddle confuses someone who can't find the answer.
Like a riddle they couldn't solve, the wooden horse baffled the Trojans and became their undoing.
The Trojans were baffled by the wooden horse as completely as someone who can't solve a riddle.
questions.hint: Think: calling the horse directly a riddle — it IS the puzzle, and their failure to solve it IS the trap

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BAFFLE?
questions.a: Their silence was a baffle — a wall of nothing that stopped every question from reaching the answer.
ch: Their silence was a baffle — a wall of nothing that stopped every question from reaching the answer.
Their silence baffled everyone the way a wall stops sound from passing through.
Like a baffle that absorbs every question, their silence prevented anyone from finding the answer.
Their silence was as baffling as a wall — every question hit it and came back unanswered.
questions.hint: Think: calling their silence directly a baffle — it IS the device that blocks, stops every attempt at understanding

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BAFFLE?
questions.a: The code was a labyrinth of symbols — it baffled every expert who entered and offered no exit.
ch: The code was a labyrinth of symbols — it baffled every expert who entered and offered no exit.
The code baffled experts the way a labyrinth confuses those who enter without a map.
Like a labyrinth with no exit, the code baffled every expert who tried to find a way through.
The experts were baffled by the code as completely as someone lost in a labyrinth.
questions.hint: Think: calling the code directly a labyrinth — it IS the maze, and to be baffled IS to be lost inside it

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BLUNDER?
questions.a: Hauling the horse inside was Troy's great blunder — the door they opened for their own destruction.
ch: Hauling the horse inside was Troy's great blunder — the door they opened for their own destruction.
Hauling the horse inside was as much a blunder as opening a door to let in an enemy.
Like a door thrown open to destruction, hauling the horse inside was the Trojans' great blunder.
The Trojans blundered when they hauled the horse inside, as if they had opened a door for their enemies.
questions.hint: Think: calling the blunder directly a door — it IS the opening through which destruction entered

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using BLUNDER?
questions.a: Every blunder is a teacher — harsh, unscheduled, and impossible to ignore.
ch: Every blunder is a teacher — harsh, unscheduled, and impossible to ignore.
Every blunder teaches you something, like a harsh teacher you didn't choose.
Like a teacher who arrives without warning, every blunder forces you to learn something.
A blunder is as much a teacher as a classroom lesson — harsh but instructive.
questions.hint: Think: calling every blunder directly a teacher — it IS the instructor, and the lesson IS the consequence

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using COLOSSAL?
questions.a: The Trojan Horse was a colossal deception — a monument to the idea that the biggest threats wear the most beautiful disguises.
ch: The Trojan Horse was a colossal deception — a monument to the idea that the biggest threats wear the most beautiful disguises.
The Trojan Horse was as colossal a deception as any monument erected to a false idea.
Like a colossal monument to deception, the wooden horse announced its purpose to everyone but its victims.
The deception of the Trojan Horse was colossal — as large and impressive as any monument.
questions.hint: Think: calling the deception directly a monument — it IS the enormous structure, built to stand as an example forever

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using COLOSSAL?
questions.a: His ego was colossal architecture — grand on the outside, hollow all the way through.
ch: His ego was colossal architecture — grand on the outside, hollow all the way through.
His ego was as colossal as a massive building — impressive from outside, empty within.
Like colossal architecture, his ego was designed to impress but offered nothing inside.
His ego was colossally hollow — as grand in appearance as a great building with nothing in it.
questions.hint: Think: calling his ego directly architecture — it IS the structure, colossal and hollow, built to impress

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DETECT?
questions.a: Cassandra's gift was the ability to detect the threads of fate before anyone else could see the weaving.
ch: Cassandra's gift was the ability to detect the threads of fate before anyone else could see the weaving.
Cassandra could detect fate's patterns the way a weaver detects a flaw in the threads before anyone else.
Like someone who detects flaws in a weaving before it's complete, Cassandra saw fate before it unfolded.
Cassandra detected the threads of fate as easily as a skilled weaver detects a knot in the cloth.
questions.hint: Think: fate as a weaving — Cassandra IS the one who detects the threads before the pattern is visible to anyone else

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DETECT?
questions.a: A good detective is a human lens — always detecting what others pass over, always focusing on what others blur.
ch: A good detective is a human lens — always detecting what others pass over, always focusing on what others blur.
A good detective detects things the way a lens focuses light — bringing detail into view that others miss.
Like a lens that detects flaws invisible to the naked eye, a good detective sees what others overlook.
A detective detects things as precisely as a lens focuses on what the unaided eye cannot resolve.
questions.hint: Think: calling the detective directly a lens — they ARE the focusing instrument, and detection IS the act of bringing things into clarity

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using DETECT?
questions.a: Their loyalty was easy to detect — it was the thread running through everything they did, visible from any angle.
ch: Their loyalty was easy to detect — it was the thread running through everything they did, visible from any angle.
Their loyalty was as easy to detect as a thread woven visibly through everything they said and did.
Like a thread running through every action, their loyalty was something you could detect from any angle.
You could detect their loyalty in everything they did, the way you detect a thread woven through cloth.
questions.hint: Think: calling loyalty directly a thread — it IS the structural element, and to detect it IS to see it running through every action

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using HAUL?
questions.a: History is a weight we all haul — some carry it like a burden, others like a lantern showing where not to walk again.
ch: History is a weight we all haul — some carry it like a burden, others like a lantern showing where not to walk again.
History feels like a weight we haul with us — sometimes a burden, sometimes a tool.
Like a weight we haul forward, history can slow us down or help us find the right path.
We haul history the way we haul a heavy load — it weighs on us whether we choose to carry it or not.
questions.hint: Think: calling history directly a weight we haul — it IS the load, and the hauling IS the act of carrying the past with us

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using HAUL?
questions.a: Every generation hauls the mistakes of the last — the question is whether they haul it to study or to repeat.
ch: Every generation hauls the mistakes of the last — the question is whether they haul it to study or to repeat.
Every generation hauls the weight of the previous generation's mistakes, like cargo passed forward.
Like cargo hauled from one ship to the next, past mistakes are carried by every new generation.
Each generation hauls history's mistakes the way a ship hauls cargo — forward, whether they wish it or not.
questions.hint: Think: calling past mistakes directly cargo to be hauled — they ARE the freight, carried from one generation to the next

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using OVERPOWER?
questions.a: In the end, Greek cunning overpowered Trojan strength — the war was not won with a sword but with a gift.
ch: In the end, Greek cunning overpowered Trojan strength — the war was not won with a sword but with a gift.
In the end, Greek cunning was powerful enough to overpower the physical strength of Troy.
Like a force that overpowers through cleverness rather than strength, the Greeks won with a gift, not a sword.
The Greeks used cunning to overpower Troy in the same way that intelligence overpowers brute force.
questions.hint: Think: cunning IS the overpowering force — it IS the weapon that defeats, and the gift IS that weapon

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using OVERPOWER?
questions.a: Silence can overpower a room — fill it completely with nothing until every voice inside goes quiet.
ch: Silence can overpower a room — fill it completely with nothing until every voice inside goes quiet.
Silence overpowers a room the way a strong presence dominates every conversation within it.
Like a force that overpowers everything in its path, silence fills a room and quiets every voice.
A powerful silence can overpower conversation the way one dominant voice overpowers all the others.
questions.hint: Think: calling silence an overpowering force — it IS the dominant presence, and to overpower IS to fill and silence everything else

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using REJOICE?
questions.a: The Trojans' rejoicing was a fire lit too soon — it consumed the night they needed to stay watch.
ch: The Trojans' rejoicing was a fire lit too soon — it consumed the night they needed to stay watch.
The Trojans rejoiced too soon, like someone who lights a fire before they've made sure it's safe.
Like a fire lit before the danger had passed, their premature rejoicing consumed the night they needed.
Their rejoicing was as dangerous as a fire — lit at the wrong moment, it burned away their vigilance.
questions.hint: Think: calling their rejoicing directly a fire lit too soon — it IS the thing that consumed the night, that burned their watchfulness

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using REJOICE?
questions.a: To rejoice is to give the whole self over to the moment — to become nothing but the fact of being glad.
ch: To rejoice is to give the whole self over to the moment — to become nothing but the fact of being glad.
To rejoice is to feel so glad that you give yourself completely to the happiness of the moment.
Like giving yourself over to the moment, to rejoice is to be nothing but gladness for a time.
Rejoicing means surrendering to happiness the way you give yourself over to a moment of pure joy.
questions.hint: Think: calling rejoicing directly a surrender of the self — to rejoice IS to become entirely the emotion

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using REJOICE?
questions.a: Their relief was a rejoicing that swept through the city like water released from a dam — sudden, total, unstoppable.
ch: Their relief was a rejoicing that swept through the city like water released from a dam — sudden, total, unstoppable.
Their relief felt like a rejoicing that swept through the city the way water bursts from a dam.
Like water released from a dam, their rejoicing was sudden and impossible to stop once it started.
Their rejoicing swept through the city the way water rushes from a broken dam — all at once, everywhere.
questions.hint: Think: calling their relief directly a rejoicing, and the rejoicing directly a flood — the emotion IS the water, unstoppable and total

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SCOFF?
questions.a: Every scoff is a closed door — you mock what you don't understand, and in doing so guarantee you never will.
ch: Every scoff is a closed door — you mock what you don't understand, and in doing so guarantee you never will.
Every scoff is as final as a door closed against understanding — once you mock, you stop learning.
Like a door closed against understanding, a scoff shuts out whatever you refuse to take seriously.
To scoff at something is to close a door on it — you end any chance of understanding by mocking it.
questions.hint: Think: calling every scoff directly a closed door — it IS the barrier, and to scoff IS to shut out all possibility of understanding

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SCOFF?
questions.a: History's greatest scoffers are its greatest footnotes — remembered only for what they refused to believe.
ch: History's greatest scoffers are its greatest footnotes — remembered only for what they refused to believe.
History's greatest scoffers are like footnotes — noted only for what they dismissed rather than what they built.
Like footnotes in the story of progress, those who scoffed are remembered only for what they rejected.
The people who scoffed at new ideas became footnotes — history remembers them for what they refused to believe.
questions.hint: Think: calling scoffers directly footnotes — they ARE the margin notes, remembered only for their dismissal

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SENTINEL?
questions.a: Memory is the sentinel of identity — it stands at the gate of who you are and decides what enters and what is turned away.
ch: Memory is the sentinel of identity — it stands at the gate of who you are and decides what enters and what is turned away.
Memory works like a sentinel for identity — it guards what you keep and turns away what doesn't belong.
Like a sentinel standing at the gate, memory decides what becomes part of who you are.
Your memory is as much a sentinel as any guard — it keeps some things in and turns others away.
questions.hint: Think: calling memory directly a sentinel — it IS the guard, and identity IS the city being protected

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SENTINEL?
questions.a: Conscience is an internal sentinel — it cannot be bribed, cannot be relieved of duty, and never truly sleeps.
ch: Conscience is an internal sentinel — it cannot be bribed, cannot be relieved of duty, and never truly sleeps.
Conscience works like a sentinel inside you — it watches everything you do and cannot be dismissed.
Like a sentinel who never leaves their post, conscience keeps watch on every action and cannot be bribed.
Your conscience is as persistent as a sentinel — it watches from inside and is impossible to dismiss.
questions.hint: Think: calling conscience directly a sentinel — it IS the guard, posted inside, impossible to corrupt or dismiss

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SENTINEL?
questions.a: The old oak at the end of the drive was a sentinel — it had stood there so long it had grown into the shape of watching.
ch: The old oak at the end of the drive was a sentinel — it had stood there so long it had grown into the shape of watching.
The old oak looked like a sentinel — it stood at the end of the drive as if placed there to watch.
Like a sentinel that has been on duty too long, the old oak had grown into the posture of permanent watch.
The old oak served as a sentinel at the end of the drive — rooted there and impossible to pass unnoticed.
questions.hint: Think: calling the oak directly a sentinel — it IS the guard, and time has shaped it into permanent watchfulness

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SIEGE?
questions.a: Grief is a siege — it surrounds you completely, cuts off all supply lines to joy, and waits for you to exhaust yourself.
ch: Grief is a siege — it surrounds you completely, cuts off all supply lines to joy, and waits for you to exhaust yourself.
Grief works like a siege — it surrounds you and cuts you off from everything that might bring comfort.
Like a siege that surrounds a city, grief cuts off every supply line to joy and waits you out.
Grief feels like being under siege — surrounded, cut off, with nothing coming in and no way out.
questions.hint: Think: calling grief directly a siege — it IS the encirclement, and joy IS the supply line that gets cut

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SIEGE?
questions.a: The silence before the verdict was a siege — pressing in from every side, making the air impossible to breathe.
ch: The silence before the verdict was a siege — pressing in from every side, making the air impossible to breathe.
The silence before the verdict felt like a siege — it pressed in from every direction with nowhere to escape.
Like a siege closing in from every direction, the silence before the verdict made everything feel impossible.
That silence was as much a siege as any army — it surrounded and pressed in until there was no room left.
questions.hint: Think: calling the silence directly a siege — it IS the encirclement, pressing from every side

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SINISTER?
questions.a: The wooden horse was sinister perfection — a gift that held its own army, a welcome that carried its own destruction.
ch: The wooden horse was sinister perfection — a gift that held its own army, a welcome that carried its own destruction.
The wooden horse was sinister in a perfect way — a gift containing soldiers, a welcome hiding destruction.
Like sinister perfection in physical form, the wooden horse was a gift that concealed its own army.
The wooden horse was perfectly sinister — as carefully designed to cause harm as anything ever built.
questions.hint: Think: calling the horse directly sinister perfection — it IS the perfected form of evil disguised as a gift

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using SINISTER?
questions.a: His patience was sinister — not the patience of someone who can wait, but of someone who has already decided the outcome.
ch: His patience was sinister — not the patience of someone who can wait, but of someone who has already decided the outcome.
His patience felt sinister — it was the patience of someone who had already decided how everything would end.
Like something sinister, his patience didn't feel like waiting — it felt like certainty of a bad outcome.
His patience was as sinister as a plan already decided — not waiting, but counting down.
questions.hint: Think: calling his patience directly sinister — it IS the threatening quality, patience as a form of certain, predatory confidence

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using VICTOR?
questions.a: Greece was ultimately not the victor of the Trojan War — history was, and it has been replaying the story ever since.
ch: Greece was ultimately not the victor of the Trojan War — history was, and it has been replaying the story ever since.
Greece was not really the victor in any lasting sense — history has outlasted the story of every winner.
Like a victor that outlasts all others, history has been retelling the Trojan War for thousands of years.
The real victor of the Trojan War was history itself — it kept the story alive long after both sides were gone.
questions.hint: Think: calling history directly the victor — it IS the true winner, the one that outlasts and preserves everything

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using VICTOR?
questions.a: Every victor carries the echo of the defeated — a triumph without that weight is hollow at the center.
ch: Every victor carries the echo of the defeated — a triumph without that weight is hollow at the center.
Every victor should remember the defeated — a triumph that ignores the cost is hollow.
Like an echo carried forward, the memory of the defeated follows every victor.
A victor that forgets the defeated is like a triumph hollow at its center — missing the weight that gives it meaning.
questions.hint: Think: calling what the victor carries an echo — it IS the sound that follows every win, the presence of the defeated

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using VICTOR?
questions.a: The real victor in any struggle is the one still learning after the battle ends — the one who takes more from the fight than the prize.
ch: The real victor in any struggle is the one still learning after the battle ends — the one who takes more from the fight than the prize.
The real victor is not the one who wins but the one who learns more from the fight than the prize could ever teach.
Like a victor who carries more than the trophy, the true winner is the one who learns from the struggle.
The one who keeps learning after winning is more of a victor than the one who only carries away the prize.
questions.hint: Think: calling the learner directly the real victor — growth IS the victory, more than any trophy or prize

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using WOE?
questions.a: Troy's woe was a city-shaped wound — it bled not blood but people, culture, and memory, until nothing was left to carry the name.
ch: Troy's woe was a city-shaped wound — it bled not blood but people, culture, and memory, until nothing was left to carry the name.
Troy's woe was as deep as a wound — it bled out everything that had made the city what it was.
Like a wound that bleeds out people and culture, Troy's woe left nothing standing to carry its name forward.
The woe of Troy was as deep as a wound — it drained the city of everything until only the name remained.
questions.hint: Think: calling Troy's woe directly a wound — it IS the injury, and everything lost IS what the wound bled away

questions.q: Which sentence is a metaphor using WOE?
questions.a: Woe is the tax that foolishness charges — paid in suffering, with interest, long after the original mistake.
ch: Woe is the tax that foolishness charges — paid in suffering, with interest, long after the original mistake.
Woe feels like a tax charged for foolishness — paid in suffering, often long after the mistake was made.
Like a tax charged on every foolish decision, woe arrives after the blunder and must be paid in suffering.
Foolishness charges woe as its tax — you pay it in suffering, often long after you've forgotten the original mistake.
questions.hint: Think: calling woe directly a tax — it IS the payment charged, and foolishness IS the thing that charges it

questions.q: GLARE (v., to shine intensely) most nearly means:

questions.q: PROMINENT most nearly means:
questions.hint: Prominent means standing out or well-known — notable

questions.q: Which word is a synonym for PROMINENT?
questions.hint: Conspicuous means easy to notice — like prominent

questions.q: QUENCH most nearly means:
questions.hint: To quench is to satisfy a thirst or put out a fire — to satisfy

questions.q: RIGOR most nearly means:
questions.hint: Rigor means harsh conditions or strictness — hardship

questions.q: Which word is a synonym for RIGOR?
questions.hint: Severity means extreme harshness — like rigor

questions.q: RIGOR is closest in meaning to:

questions.q: SEAR most nearly means:
questions.hint: To sear is to burn or wither with intense heat — to scorch

questions.q: Which word is a synonym for SEAR?
questions.hint: To wither is to dry up and shrink — one meaning of sear

questions.hint: An oppressor uses power to crush and control others — like a tyrant

questions.q: TYRANT is closest in meaning to:
questions.hint: A despot rules with absolute cruel power — like a tyrant

questions.q: Which of the following is a synonym for TYRANT?
questions.hint: An autocrat rules with unlimited power without restraint — like a tyrant

questions.q: VANQUISH most nearly means:
questions.hint: To vanquish is to defeat someone completely — to defeat

questions.q: Which word is a synonym for VANQUISH?
questions.hint: To conquer means to overcome by force — like vanquish

questions.q: CONSENT is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of DISCREET?
questions.hint: Discreet means showing care in speech and behavior — the opposite is reckless

questions.q: DISCREET is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:

questions.q: Which word is most nearly OPPOSITE to DISCREET?
questions.hint: Tactless means lacking care in what you say — the opposite of discreet

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of ENGROSS?
questions.hint: To engross is to hold someone's complete attention — the opposite leaves them bored

questions.q: ENGROSS is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: To distract is to pull attention away — the opposite of engross which pulls it in

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of ESTEEM?
questions.hint: Esteem means to think highly of someone — scorn means to think very poorly of them

questions.q: ESTEEM is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: To despise someone is to dislike them deeply — the opposite of esteem

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of EXAGGERATE?
questions.hint: To exaggerate is to make something bigger than it is — the opposite makes it smaller

questions.q: EXAGGERATE is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: Minimize means to make seem less important than it is — the opposite of exaggerate

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of EXTENSIVE?
questions.hint: Extensive means wide in scope — the opposite is limited or narrow

questions.q: EXTENSIVE is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: Narrow means covering only a small range — the opposite of extensive

questions.q: Which word is most nearly OPPOSITE to EXTENSIVE?
questions.hint: Confined means restricted to a small area — the opposite of extensive

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of FANTASTIC?
questions.hint: Fantastic means extraordinary — the opposite is something everyday and ordinary

questions.q: FANTASTIC is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: Mundane means ordinary and not interesting — the opposite of fantastic

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of INTRIGUE (v.)?
questions.hint: To intrigue is to fascinate someone — the opposite fails to interest them at all

questions.q: INTRIGUE (v.) is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: To repel means to drive away — the opposite of intriguing which draws people in

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of MARVEL (v.)?
questions.hint: To marvel is to be filled with wonder — the opposite pays no attention at all

questions.q: MARVEL (n.) is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: A marvel fills you with wonder — the opposite is something that disappoints

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of MISSION?
questions.hint: A mission involves clear purpose and direction — the opposite lacks any goal

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of OPPORTUNITY?
questions.hint: An opportunity is a favorable condition — an obstacle is something that prevents progress

questions.q: OPPORTUNITY is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: A barrier blocks your way — the opposite of an opportunity which opens the way

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of RELINQUISH?
questions.hint: To relinquish is to give up — the opposite is to keep or retain

questions.q: RELINQUISH is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: To grasp is to hold tightly — the opposite of relinquish which means to let go

questions.q: Which word is most nearly OPPOSITE to RELINQUISH?
questions.hint: To seize means to take hold firmly — the opposite of giving something up

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of TYRANT?
questions.hint: A tyrant takes power and crushes others — a servant gives up power to help others

questions.q: TYRANT is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: A liberator frees people — the opposite of a tyrant who enslaves them

questions.q: Which word is the OPPOSITE of VANQUISH?
questions.hint: To vanquish means to defeat completely — the opposite is to give up entirely

questions.q: VANQUISH is most nearly OPPOSITE in meaning to:
questions.hint: To yield is to give way or submit — the opposite of vanquishing which means to win

questions.q: Which word is most nearly OPPOSITE to VANQUISH?
questions.hint: To succumb means to give in to a greater force — the opposite of vanquish

questions.q: Which word is most nearly OPPOSITE to OPPORTUNITY?
questions.hint: A hindrance blocks your path — the opposite of an opportunity which opens it
